topics.title: Introduction to Electrical Circuits: Circuit Concept, Types of Elements, Source Transformation, Voltage-Current Relationship for Passive Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT631 - NETWORK AND LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Electrical Circuits: Circuit Concept, Types of Elements, Source Transformation, Voltage-Current Relationship for Passive Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Electrical Circuit?

An **electrical circuit** is a closed conducting path that allows the flow of electric current from a source of electrical energy (such as a battery or generator), through various interconnected components (resistors, inductors, capacitors), and back to the source. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a circuit is a **mathematical model** that represents the interconnection of lumped electrical elements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):**
> An *electric circuit* is the combination of a number of electrical elements (active and passive) connected in a closed path, so that electric current can flow through them to perform a specific function such as signal processing or power transfer.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Water-Pipe Model

Imagine a **water distribution system** in your home:

- The **water tank (overhead)** = Voltage source (provides pressure)
- The **water flowing through the pipe** = Electric current
- The **narrow section of pipe** (which restricts flow) = Resistor
- The **flexible hose (inertia of water)** = Inductor
- The **expansion chamber (stores water)** = Capacitor
- The **closed loop of pipes** = Closed circuit
- A **broken pipe** = Open circuit

Just as water needs a closed pipe to flow continuously, electric current needs a closed circuit to flow. The "pressure" (voltage) drives the "flow" (current) through "restrictions" (resistors).

---

## 1.2 Circuit Concept — Topology Terminology

| Term | KTU Board Definition |
|---|---|
| **Node** | A point of interconnection between two or more elements (a junction) |
| **Branch** | A single element (resistor, source, etc.) connected between two nodes |
| **Loop** | Any closed path in a circuit |
| **Mesh** | A loop that does not contain any other loop inside it (smallest loop) |
| **Essential Node** | A node where 3 or more elements meet (Principal Node) |
| **Essential Branch** | A branch that connects two essential nodes |

> [!NOTE]
> A **planar circuit** is one that can be drawn on a flat surface without any branch crossing another. For such circuits, **Number of meshes = Number of independent loops**.

---

## 1.3 Types of Circuit Elements

Elements are classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

### A. Active vs Passive Elements

- **Active Element**: Delivers energy to the circuit (sources — battery, generator). Cannot be described by a single passive v-i relation.
- **Passive Element**: Receives/absorbs energy (or stores it temporarily). Always obeys $v \cdot i \geq 0$ for some region.

### B. Independent vs Dependent (Controlled) Sources

- **Independent Source**: Magnitude is set by the source itself, independent of any circuit variable.
- **Dependent Source**: Magnitude is controlled by another voltage or current elsewhere in the circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Four types of dependent sources exist in KTU syllabus:
> 1. **VCVS** — Voltage Controlled Voltage Source
> 2. **VCCS** — Voltage Controlled Current Source
> 3. **CCVS** — Current Controlled Voltage Source
> 4. **CCCS** — Current Controlled Current Source

---

## 1.4 Source Transformation — The Concept

**Source Transformation** is the technique of replacing a practical voltage source (with series resistance) by an equivalent practical current source (with parallel resistance), and vice versa. The equivalence is based on matching the open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current at the external terminals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** A 9V battery with internal resistance is like a powerful but "tired" engine. It can be re-modelled as an ideal current source in parallel with the same resistance — both deliver the same power to any external load.

---

## 1.5 Voltage-Current Relationship for Passive Elements

The three fundamental passive elements obey characteristic V-I equations:

| Element | Voltage-Current (V-I) Relation | Energy Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Resistor ($R$) | $v = iR$ (Ohm's Law) | Dissipates energy as heat |
| Inductor ($L$) | $v = L\,\dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Stores energy in magnetic field |
| Capacitor ($C$) | $i = C\,\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | Stores energy in electric field |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:** Resistor is **instantaneous** (algebraic), while $L$ and $C$ are **dynamic** (differential) elements. This makes $L$ and $C$ the sources of *memory* in circuits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Circuit Topological Framework

A circuit is analysed using two complementary models:

1. **Geometric/Topological Model** — Describes *how* elements are connected (nodes, branches, loops).
2. **Algebraic Model** — Describes *what* each element does mathematically (KVL, KCL, element equations).

### Fundamental Counting Laws (KTU High-Yield)

For any planar connected circuit with **b** branches, **n** nodes, and **m** meshes (independent loops):

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= n - 1 + m \quad &\text{(Euler's relation for connected planar circuits)}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **KCL** gives $n - 1$ independent equations (one node is the reference/ground).
- **KVL** gives $m$ independent mesh equations.
- Total independent equations = $(n - 1) + m = b$ → system is solvable.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Elements — Detailed Hierarchy

### 2.2.1 Active Elements (Sources)

An **ideal independent voltage source** maintains a fixed voltage across its terminals regardless of the current drawn. Symbol: circle with $\pm$ or diamond with $+/-$ in KTU diagrams.

An **ideal independent current source** delivers a fixed current regardless of the voltage across it.

A **practical source** includes an internal resistance:
- Practical voltage source = ideal voltage source $V_s$ **in series** with resistance $R_s$
- Practical current source = ideal current source $I_s$ **in parallel** with resistance $R_p$

### 2.2.2 Dependent (Controlled) Sources — Detailed

Dependent sources model **transistors, op-amps, and active filters**. They are not physical batteries — they have *no independent existence*; their value depends on a controlling variable.

| Type | Controlling Variable | Output | Equation Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| VCVS | Voltage $v_x$ | Voltage $v_y$ | $v_y = \mu\,v_x$ |
| VCCS | Voltage $v_x$ | Current $i_y$ | $i_y = g_m\,v_x$ |
| CCVS | Current $i_x$ | Voltage $v_y$ | $v_y = r_m\,i_x$ |
| CCCS | Current $i_x$ | Current $i_y$ | $i_y = \beta\,i_x$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Dependent sources **cannot** be combined via source transformation unless the controlling variable remains external to the transformation.

---

## 2.3 Source Transformation — Full Theoretical Treatment

### 2.3.1 Voltage Source → Current Source

A practical voltage source $(V_s$ in series with $R_s)$ is replaced by a practical current source $(I_s$ in parallel with $R_s)$ using:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_s = \frac{V_s}{R_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.2 Current Source → Voltage Source

A practical current source $(I_s$ in parallel with $R_p)$ is replaced by a practical voltage source $(V_s$ in series with $R_p)$ using:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s = I_s \cdot R_p
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial KTU Rule:** The resistance value is **identical** in both forms. Only the topology (series vs parallel with respect to the source) changes.

### 2.3.3 Validity Conditions

Source transformation is valid when:
1. The source and resistance are at the **same pair of terminals**.
2. The circuit external to these terminals sees the **same v-i characteristics** (matched open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current).
3. The transformation preserves **power dissipation in $R$** only — not the source power.

---

## 2.4 V-I Relationship for Passive Elements — Detailed

### 2.4.1 Resistor (R)

Ohm's Law in both directions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v(t) &= R \cdot i(t) \\
i(t) &= G \cdot v(t) \quad \text{where } G = \frac{1}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Power absorbed:** $P = i^2 R = v^2 / R = vi$

**Energy:** Always dissipated, never stored. $W = \int_0^t i^2 R \, d\tau \geq 0$

### 2.4.2 Inductor (L)

For an inductor with $N$ turns, flux $\phi$, and current $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v(t) &= L \frac{di(t)}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

Current from voltage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i(t) &= i(0^-) + \frac{1}{L} \int_0^t v(\tau)\,d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Power:** $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$

**Energy stored:** $W_L = \dfrac{1}{2} L\, i^2(t)$

> [!NOTE]
> An inductor **opposes changes in current**. In steady DC, $v = 0$ → inductor behaves as a **short circuit**.

### 2.4.3 Capacitor (C)

For a capacitor with charge $q$ and voltage $v$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i(t) &= C \frac{dv(t)}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

Voltage from current:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v(t) &= v(0^-) + \frac{1}{C} \int_0^t i(\tau)\,d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Charge stored:** $q = Cv$

**Energy stored:** $W_C = \dfrac{1}{2} C\, v^2(t)$

> [!NOTE]
> A capacitor **opposes changes in voltage**. In steady DC, $i = 0$ → capacitor behaves as an **open circuit**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the most frequently tested content in KTU Module 1.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law | $v = iR$ | $R$ in Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 2 | Conductance | $G = 1/R$ | Siemens (S) |
| 3 | Resistor Power | $P = vi = i^2R = v^2/R$ | Watts (W) |
| 4 | Inductor V-I | $v = L\,di/dt$ | $L$ in Henry (H) |
| 5 | Inductor Energy | $W_L = \tfrac{1}{2}Li^2$ | Joules (J) |
| 6 | Capacitor V-I | $i = C\,dv/dt$ | $C$ in Farad (F) |
| 7 | Capacitor Energy | $W_C = \tfrac{1}{2}Cv^2$ | Joules (J) |
| 8 | Source Transformation (V→I) | $I_s = V_s/R_s$ | Series $R$ → Parallel $R$ |
| 9 | Source Transformation (I→V) | $V_s = I_s R_p$ | Parallel $R$ → Series $R$ |
| 10 | Open-Ckt Voltage (Practical V-source) | $V_{oc} = V_s$ | Across terminals |
| 11 | Short-Ckt Current (Practical I-source) | $I_{sc} = I_s$ | Through terminals |
| 12 | Number of meshes | $m = b - n + 1$ | Planar circuits |
| 13 | KCL | $\sum i_{in} = \sum i_{out}$ | At each node |
| 14 | KVL | $\sum v_{rise} = \sum v_{drop}$ | Around any loop |
| 15 | VCVS gain | $v_y = \mu v_x$ | Dimensionless |
| 16 | CCCS gain | $i_y = \beta i_x$ | Dimensionless |
| 17 | VCCS gain | $i_y = g_m v_x$ | Siemens |
| 18 | CCVS gain | $v_y = r_m i_x$ | Ohms |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Source transformation** is the foundation of **Norton and Thévenin equivalent circuits**, which are essential in power system fault analysis, amplifier small-signal modelling, and load-line analysis in BJT circuits.
- **R, L, C V-I relations** underpin the design of every filter (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass), every oscillator, every power supply, and every RF impedance-matching network.
- **Dependent sources** model transistors in small-signal analysis (BJT → CCCS/CCVS, FET → VCCS).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Source Transformation Equivalence

**Goal:** Show that a practical voltage source $(V_s, R_s)$ in series with terminals $a$–$b$ delivers the *same* terminal v-i relation as a practical current source $(I_s, R_p)$ in parallel.

### Step 1 — Terminal v-i Relation of the Practical Voltage Source

Let a load resistance $R_L$ be connected across terminals $a$–$b$.

Using KVL around the loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= i R_s + v_L
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for the terminal voltage $v_L$ (voltage across $a$–$b$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_L &= V_s - i R_s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Short-Circuit Current of Practical Voltage Source

Setting $v_L = 0$ (short circuit between $a$ and $b$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sc} &= \frac{V_s}{R_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Open-Circuit Voltage of Practical Voltage Source

Setting $i = 0$ (open circuit at $a$–$b$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oc} &= V_s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Terminal v-i Relation of the Practical Current Source

Now consider a practical current source $I_s$ with internal resistance $R_p$ in parallel. Connect load $R_L$ at the terminals.

KCL at the top node gives the terminal voltage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_s &= i_L + i_{Rp} \\
I_s &= \frac{v_L}{R_L} + \frac{v_L}{R_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $v_L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_L &= I_s \left( \frac{R_L R_p}{R_L + R_p} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The current into the load is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_L &= \frac{v_L}{R_L} = I_s \cdot \frac{R_p}{R_L + R_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Equating Short-Circuit Currents

When $R_L = 0$ (short circuit), the voltage source gave $I_{sc} = V_s/R_s$. For the current source model with $R_L = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_L \Big\vert_{R_L = 0} &= I_s
\end{aligned}
$$

For equivalence, these must be equal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_s &= \frac{V_s}{R_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Equating Open-Circuit Voltages

When $R_L \to \infty$, the current source gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oc} &= I_s \cdot R_p
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $I_s = V_s / R_s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oc} &= \frac{V_s}{R_s} \cdot R_p
\end{aligned}
$$

For this to equal $V_s$ (the open-circuit voltage of the voltage source model):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \frac{V_s}{R_s} \cdot R_p \implies R_p = R_s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Conclusion

$$
\boxed{\,I_s = \frac{V_s}{R_s} \quad \text{and} \quad R_p = R_s\,}
$$

This is the **fundamental source-transformation equation** in KTU Module 1.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Power in a Resistor from V-I Relation

Starting from Ohm's Law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v(t) &= R \cdot i(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Power absorbed by the resistor at any instant $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(t) &= v(t) \cdot i(t) \\
p(t) &= \bigl(R\, i(t)\bigr) \cdot i(t) \\
p(t) &= R\, i^2(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $i(t) = v(t)/R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(t) &= v(t) \cdot \frac{v(t)}{R} = \frac{v^2(t)}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R \geq 0$, $p(t) \geq 0$ always — confirming that the resistor is a **strictly passive**, dissipative element.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Energy Stored in an Inductor

Power delivered to the inductor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(t) &= v(t) \cdot i(t) = \left(L\,\frac{di}{dt}\right) i(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Energy stored from $t = 0$ to $t = T$, assuming $i(0) = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_L(T) &= \int_0^T p(t)\,dt = \int_0^T L\,\frac{di}{dt}\,i\,dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the substitution $u = i$, $du = (di/dt)\,dt$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_L(T) &= L \int_{0}^{i(T)} u\,du \\
W_L(T) &= \frac{1}{2} L\, i^2(T)
\end{aligned}
$$

Generalised (non-zero initial current):

$$
\boxed{\,W_L(t) = \frac{1}{2} L\, \bigl[i^2(t) - i^2(0^-)\bigr]\,}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Capacitor

Power delivered to the capacitor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(t) &= v(t) \cdot i(t) = v(t) \left(C\,\frac{dv}{dt}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Energy stored from $v(0) = 0$ to $v(T)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_C(T) &= \int_0^T p(t)\,dt = \int_0^T C\, v(t)\,\frac{dv}{dt}\,dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $u = v$, $du = (dv/dt)\,dt$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_C(T) &= C \int_{0}^{v(T)} u\,du \\
W_C(T) &= \frac{1}{2} C\, v^2(T)
\end{aligned}
$$

Generalised (non-zero initial voltage):

$$
\boxed{\,W_C(t) = \frac{1}{2} C\, \bigl[v^2(t) - v^2(0^-)\bigr]\,}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Passive Elements

| Property | Resistor $R$ | Inductor $L$ | Capacitor $C$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| V-I (instant) | $v = iR$ | $v = L\,\dfrac{di}{dt}$ | $i = C\,\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ |
| I-V (integral) | $i = v/R$ | $i = \dfrac{1}{L}\int v\,dt + i_0$ | $v = \dfrac{1}{C}\int i\,dt + v_0$ |
| Energy stored | 0 (dissipated) | $\tfrac{1}{2}Li^2$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}Cv^2$ |
| DC Steady-state | Same $R$ | Short circuit ($v = 0$) | Open circuit ($i = 0$) |
| AC behaviour | Impedance $R$ | $j\omega L$ | $1/(j\omega C)$ |
| Memory | No (memoryless) | Yes (stores current history) | Yes (stores voltage history) |
| Continuity | — | $i_L$ cannot jump | $v_C$ cannot jump |
| Power sign | $P \geq 0$ always | Can be $\pm$ | Can be $\pm$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In DC steady-state, you *must* replace an inductor by a **short circuit** and a capacitor by an **open circuit** before applying KCL/KVL — this is the most frequent trick in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: Source Transformation

**Problem:** A practical voltage source has $V_s = 12\,\text{V}$ and internal resistance $R_s = 4\,\Omega$. Transform it to its equivalent current source form.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the elements:**

$$
V_s = 12\,\text{V}, \qquad R_s = 4\,\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the transformation formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_s &= \frac{V_s}{R_s} = \frac{12}{4} = 3\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — The parallel resistance is the same:**

$$
R_p = R_s = 4\,\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Verify with open-circuit voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oc} = I_s \cdot R_p = 3 \times 4 = 12\,\text{V} \quad &\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify with short-circuit current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sc} = \frac{V_s}{R_s} = 3\,\text{A} \quad &\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The equivalent practical current source is $I_s = 3\,\text{A}$ in parallel with $R_p = 4\,\Omega$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Diagram — Circuit Elements

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Circuit Elements] --> B[Active]
    A --> C[Passive]

    B --> B1[Independent Sources]
    B --> B2[Dependent Sources]

    B1 --> B1a[Ideal Voltage Source V_s]
    B1 --> B1b[Ideal Current Source I_s]

    B2 --> B2a[VCVS]
    B2 --> B2b[VCCS]
    B2 --> B2c[CCVS]
    B2 --> B2d[CCCS]

    C --> C1[Resistor R]
    C --> C2[Inductor L]
    C --> C3[Capacitor C]

    C1 --> C1a[Memoryless]
    C2 --> C2a[Energy Storage Magnetic]
    C3 --> C3a[Energy Storage Electric]
```

## 4.2 Source Transformation — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph V_FORM["Practical Voltage Source"]
        V1[Vs] -->|series| R1[Rs]
        R1 --> T1[Terminal a]
        V1 --> T2[Terminal b]
    end

    V_FORM -. V_s / R_s .-> I_FORM

    subgraph I_FORM["Practical Current Source"]
        I1[Is] -->|parallel| R2[Rp = Rs]
        I1 --> T3[Terminal a]
        R2 --> T4[Terminal b]
    end
```

## 4.3 V-I Relationship — Behavioural Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Apply V or I input] --> Q{Element Type?}

    Q -->|Resistor| R[Instantaneous Ohm's Law: v = iR]
    Q -->|Inductor| L[Differential: v = L di/dt, stores in magnetic field]
    Q -->|Capacitor| C[Differential: i = C dv/dt, stores in electric field]

    R --> R1[Power = i squared times R, dissipated as heat]
    L --> L1[Energy = 1/2 L i squared, stored]
    C --> C1[Energy = 1/2 C v squared, stored]

    L1 --> L2[DC Steady: Short Circuit]
    C1 --> C2[DC Steady: Open Circuit]
```

## 4.4 Circuit Topology — Node, Branch, Mesh Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S1["Example: 2-Mesh Circuit Topology"]
        N1[Node 1 - Essential Node] ---|Branch R1| N2[Node 2]
        N2 ---|Branch V_s| N3[Node 3]
        N3 ---|Branch R2| N1
        N2 ---|Branch R3| N4[Node 4 - Essential Node]
        N4 ---|Branch R4| N3
    end

    M1[Mesh 1: N1-R1-N2-Vs-N3-R2 loop]
    M2[Mesh 2: N2-R3-N4-R4-N3 loop]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above shows a circuit with **2 essential nodes** (N2 and N4) and **2 meshes**. By Euler's formula: $b = n - 1 + m = 2 - 1 + 2 = 3$... but actually shown has **5 branches**. The remaining branches are essential branches. Always count carefully in KTU numerical problems.

## 4.5 Dependent Source — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CV[Controlling Variable v_x or i_x] --> AM[Amplifier / Gain Block]
    AM --> CO[Controlled Output v_y or i_y]

    AM -->|mu| A1[VCVS - Voltage Out]
    AM -->|g_m| A2[VCCS - Current Out]
    AM -->|r_m| A3[CCVS - Voltage Out]
    AM -->|beta| A4[CCCS - Current Out]

    CV -.feeds back to same circuit.-> CO
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Define:**
(i) An active element and a passive element.
(ii) A dependent source. Give one example of a practical device modelled by it.

**Model Answer:**

(i) An **active element** is one that is capable of delivering energy to the rest of the circuit (e.g., a battery or a generator). Its v-i characteristic is not constrained to absorb power; it can supply power. A **passive element** absorbs and/or stores energy and always satisfies $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) \geq 0$ for at least part of its operating range. Examples: resistor (dissipative), inductor and capacitor (energy-storing).

(ii) A **dependent (controlled) source** is an active element whose magnitude (voltage or current) is controlled by another voltage or current elsewhere in the circuit. It has no independent existence. Example: A bipolar junction transistor (BJT) in the active region is commonly modelled as a **CCCS** with $i_c = \beta i_b$.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024 Rubric):**
- Definition of active/passive: **1.5 marks**
- Dependent source + example: **1.5 marks**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**State the V-I relationships for (a) a resistor, (b) an inductor, and (c) a capacitor. Mention one energy-related property of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Resistor:** $v(t) = R\, i(t)$ — instantaneous, memoryless. Energy is **dissipated** as heat; power $P = i^2 R \geq 0$.

**(b) Inductor:** $v(t) = L\,\dfrac{di}{dt}$ — dynamic. Energy is **stored** in the magnetic field; $W_L = \tfrac{1}{2}L i^2$.

**(c) Capacitor:** $i(t) = C\,\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ — dynamic. Energy is **stored** in the electric field; $W_C = \tfrac{1}{2}C v^2$.

**Mark Distribution:**
- (a) V-I + energy property: **1 mark**
- (b) V-I + energy property: **1 mark**
- (c) V-I + energy property: **1 mark**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for source transformation from a practical voltage source to an equivalent practical current source. State clearly the conditions for validity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A practical voltage source has an EMF of $24\,\text{V}$ and an internal resistance of $6\,\Omega$. It is connected to a load of $18\,\Omega$.
   (i) Transform the source into its Norton equivalent. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) Find the current through the load using both the original and Norton forms. Verify that the results are identical. **(4 marks)**

#### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Consider a practical voltage source consisting of an ideal source $V_s$ in series with internal resistance $R_s$, delivering current $i$ to an external load. The terminal voltage is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v = V_s - i R_s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** The open-circuit voltage is obtained by setting $i = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oc} = V_s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The short-circuit current is obtained by setting $v = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sc} = \frac{V_s}{R_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** A practical current source consists of an ideal current source $I_s$ in parallel with resistance $R_p$. Its terminal v-i relation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v = I_s R_p - i R_p \cdot \left(\frac{R_p}{R_p + R_{ext}}\right) \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

Following the standard derivation (see Section 3.1 above), for the terminal v-i characteristics to be identical:

$$
\boxed{\,I_s = \frac{V_s}{R_s} \quad \text{and} \quad R_p = R_s\,}
$$

**Validity Conditions (any 2 for full marks):**
- Transformation valid only for the external terminals $a$–$b$.
- The transformation preserves power dissipation in $R_s$ but **not** in the source itself.
- Dependent sources require special care: the controlling variable must remain unchanged by the transformation.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Norton Equivalent (3 marks):**

Given $V_s = 24\,\text{V}$, $R_s = 6\,\Omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_N &= \frac{V_s}{R_s} = \frac{24}{6} = 4\,\text{A} \\
R_N &= R_s = 6\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Norton equivalent:** $I_N = 4\,\text{A}$ in parallel with $R_N = 6\,\Omega$.

**(ii) Load Current (4 marks):**

**Using original (Thévenin) form:** Load current through $R_L = 18\,\Omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_L &= \frac{V_s}{R_s + R_L} = \frac{24}{6 + 18} = \frac{24}{24} = 1\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Using Norton form:** Current divider between $R_N$ and $R_L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_L &= I_N \cdot \frac{R_N}{R_N + R_L} = 4 \cdot \frac{6}{6 + 18} = 4 \cdot \frac{6}{24} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = 1\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods yield $i_L = 1\,\text{A}$. **Verified ✓**

**Mark Distribution:**
- (a) Derivation: **7 marks** (terminal v-i: 2, equivalence: 3, validity: 2)
- (b)(i) Norton transform: **3 marks** (formula 1, calculation 2)
- (b)(ii) Load current verification: **4 marks** (original 2, Norton 1, verification 1)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the V-I relationship of a practical voltage source and a practical current source. Define a dependent source and explain all four types (VCVS, VCCS, CCVS, CCCS) with their mathematical models. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circuit contains a resistor of $10\,\Omega$, an inductor of $0.2\,\text{H}$ with an initial current of $5\,\text{A}$, and a capacitor of $50\,\mu\text{F}$ with an initial voltage of $100\,\text{V}$.
   (i) Compute the total energy stored in the inductor and capacitor at $t = 0$. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) If the capacitor voltage is found to be $v_C(t) = 100 e^{-100 t}\,\text{V}$ for $t \geq 0$, find the current through the capacitor and verify power conservation. **(4 marks)**

#### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)

**Practical Voltage Source V-I:**
A real battery cannot maintain a perfectly constant terminal voltage; as load current increases, terminal voltage drops linearly. The v-i curve is a straight line with negative slope.

$$
v = V_s - i R_s
$$

- **Open-circuit** ($i = 0$): $v = V_s$
- **Short-circuit** ($v = 0$): $i = V_s / R_s = I_{sc}$

**Practical Current Source V-I:**
A real current source has an internal parallel resistance; terminal current decreases as voltage across terminals increases.

$$
i = I_s - \frac{v}{R_p}
$$

- **Short-circuit** ($v = 0$): $i = I_s$
- **Open-circuit** ($i = 0$): $v = I_s R_p$

**Dependent Sources:**

A **dependent source** is an active element whose output is controlled by a circuit variable (voltage or current) elsewhere in the circuit.

| Type | Equation | Units of Gain |
|---|---|---|
| VCVS | $v_y = \mu\,v_x$ | Dimensionless |
| VCCS | $i_y = g_m\,v_x$ | Siemens (S) |
| CCVS | $v_y = r_m\,i_x$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| CCCS | $i_y = \beta\,i_x$ | Dimensionless |

Practical examples: BJT → CCCS with $\beta$, op-amp → VCVS with high $\mu$, FET → VCCS with $g_m$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Energy at $t = 0$ (3 marks):**

Inductor energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_L(0) &= \frac{1}{2} L\, i_L^2(0) = \frac{1}{2}(0.2)(5)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.2)(25) = 2.5\,\text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

Capacitor energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_C(0) &= \frac{1}{2} C\, v_C^2(0) = \frac{1}{2}(50 \times 10^{-6})(100)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2}(50 \times 10^{-6})(10^4) = 0.25\,\text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total energy: $W_{total}(0) = 2.5 + 0.25 = 2.75\,\text{J}$

**(ii) Current through capacitor (4 marks):**

Given $v_C(t) = 100 e^{-100t}\,\text{V}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_C(t) &= C \frac{dv_C}{dt} = (50 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\bigl(100 e^{-100t}\bigr) \\
i_C(t) &= (50 \times 10^{-6})(-100)(100) e^{-100t} \\
i_C(t) &= (50 \times 10^{-6})(-10^4) e^{-100t} \\
i_C(t) &= -0.5\, e^{-100t}\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Power delivered to capacitor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_C(t) &= v_C(t) \cdot i_C(t) = (100 e^{-100t})(-0.5 e^{-100t}) \\
p_C(t) &= -50\, e^{-200t}\,\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

Negative sign indicates the capacitor is **delivering** stored energy to the rest of the circuit, consistent with its voltage and current decaying over time.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Source Transformation:** Students often forget to keep $R$ *identical* in both forms. Do NOT change $R$ from series to a different parallel value. Mark deduction: **-2 marks**.
> 2. **Direction of Current Source Arrow:** The arrow of $I_s$ in the current source form **must** point toward the same terminal that was $+$ in the voltage source form. Reversing this is a **fatal error** costing **full marks** on the sub-question.
> 3. **DC Steady-State Trick:** When asked for steady-state values, replace $L$ by a short circuit and $C$ by an open circuit *before* solving. Many students apply KVL with inductor and capacitor still present — losing **3 marks** or more.
> 4. **Dependent Source Transformation:** NEVER apply source transformation to a dependent source unless the controlling variable is unaffected. The examiner specifically checks this.
> 5. **Initial Conditions:** Energy calculations at $t = 0^-$ use *initial* values. Forgetting initial conditions is the **#1 mistake** in KTU Module 1 numericals.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A circuit is a **closed loop** of interconnected elements through which current can flow.
- [ ] **Node** = junction, **Branch** = element between two nodes, **Loop** = closed path, **Mesh** = smallest loop.
- [ ] Planar circuits satisfy $b = n - 1 + m$.
- [ ] **Active elements** deliver energy; **passive elements** absorb/store energy.
- [ ] **Independent sources** are self-contained; **dependent sources** are controlled by other variables.
- [ ] Four dependent source types: **VCVS, VCCS, CCVS, CCCS**.
- [ ] **Source transformation:** $I_s = V_s/R$ and $R_{parallel} = R_{series}$.
- [ ] **Resistor:** $v = iR$, **Inductor:** $v = L\,di/dt$, **Capacitor:** $i = C\,dv/dt$.
- [ ] Inductor stores $\tfrac{1}{2}Li^2$ in magnetic field; capacitor stores $\tfrac{1}{2}Cv^2$ in electric field.
- [ ] In DC steady state: **L → short circuit**, **C → open circuit**.
- [ ] Inductor current and capacitor voltage **cannot change instantaneously**.
- [ ] Source transformation preserves **terminal v-i characteristics**, not internal source power.
- [ ] Always verify source transformation with open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current.
- [ ] Resistor is **memoryless**; inductor and capacitor are **memory elements**.
- [ ] KTU frequently tests: $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$ equivalence after source transformation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Reduction Techniques: Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT631 - NETWORK AND LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Reduction Techniques: Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Network Reduction** is the systematic process of simplifying a complex electrical network into a simpler equivalent form by combining elements that are connected in **series**, **parallel**, or in **series-parallel** combinations, without altering the voltage-current (V-I) behavior at the external terminals of the network.

When two or more passive elements (Resistors, Inductors, or Capacitors) or sources share a **single common node** and carry the **same current** throughout, they are said to be in **Series Connection**. The series reduction technique replaces such a chain of elements by a single equivalent element that exhibits the same voltage drop for the same current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Identification Rule for Series Elements:**
> 1. Exactly **one path** exists for current flow.
> 2. The **same current** flows through every element.
> 3. There are **no junctions (nodes with more than two elements)** between the elements being combined.
> 4. The total voltage drop is the **algebraic sum** of individual voltage drops.

## Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Toll Booth Chain"

Imagine you are driving along a single-lane highway. There is no bypass road — your car **must** pass through Toll Booth 1, then Toll Booth 2, then Toll Booth 3. Each booth adds a small delay (analogous to voltage drop across each impedance). 

- Your car (the **current**) is the same vehicle throughout the journey — it doesn't multiply or split.
- The **total delay** is the **sum** of delays at each booth.
- For an external observer at the entry and exit points, the entire chain can be replaced by **one super-booth** that delays you by the total sum.

This is precisely how **series network reduction** works — multiple impedances in series are combined into one equivalent impedance whose value is the **algebraic sum** of the individual impedances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Series Reduction Important?**
> - It is the **first step** in analyzing any complex circuit (mesh or nodal analysis becomes trivial after reduction).
> - It is **fundamental to deriving transfer functions** of control systems (e.g., series RC, series RL, series RLC circuits).
> - It underpins the **Voltage Divider Rule (VDR)**, an essential tool in sensor circuits, bias networks, and feedback design.

## Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R$ | Resistance | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** |
| $L$ | Inductance | **Henry (H)** |
| $C$ | Capacitance | **Farad (F)** |
| $Z$ | Impedance | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** |
| $Y$ | Admittance | **Siemens (S)** |
| $V$ | Voltage | **Volt (V)** |
| $I$ | Current | **Ampere (A)** |

The angular frequency $f$ in **Hertz (Hz)** and $\omega = 2\pi f$ in **rad/s** are the standard frequency metrics used when reducing impedances.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Structured Logical Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

### Step 1: Identification of Series Branches
Before reducing, you must **scan the network** and identify nodes where **only two elements meet**. If a chain of elements is connected end-to-end with no branching node in between, they form a **series path**.

### Step 2: Application of KVL (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law)
For series-connected elements, KVL is the governing principle. The total voltage across the series combination equals the **algebraic sum** of the individual voltages.

$$V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \cdots + V_n$$

### Step 3: Substitution Using Element Laws
Each voltage is expressed in terms of current $I$ using Ohm's law (for R), the inductor V-I relation $V = L \frac{dI}{dt}$, or the capacitor relation $V = \frac{1}{C}\int I \, dt$.

### Step 4: Factoring Out the Common Quantity
Since the **same current flows through all elements**, factoring out $I$ yields the **equivalent impedance**.

### Step 5: Verification by Power Balance
The total power dissipated by the equivalent element must equal the sum of individual powers — this is the **consistency check**.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they cover 90% of the series reduction questions in KTU ESE.**

| S.No. | Configuration | Equivalent Value | Key Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Series Resistors | $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_n$ | Same $I$ through all |
| 2 | Series Inductors | $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + \cdots + L_n$ | No mutual coupling |
| 3 | Series Capacitors | $\dfrac{1}{C_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{C_1} + \dfrac{1}{C_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{C_n}$ | Reciprocal sum |
| 4 | Series Impedances | $Z_{eq} = Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_3 + \cdots + Z_n$ | Phasor / complex sum |
| 5 | Voltage Divider | $V_k = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{Z_k}{Z_{eq}}$ | Output across $Z_k$ |
| 6 | Current in Series | $I_1 = I_2 = I_3 = \cdots = I_n$ | Identity by definition |
| 7 | Series Voltage Sources | $V_{eq} = V_1 \pm V_2 \pm \cdots \pm V_n$ | Algebraic sum (signs) |
| 8 | Series Current Sources | **Not allowed directly** | Must use source transformation |

### Voltage Divider Rule (VDR) — Detailed Form

For two impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ in series with input voltage $V_{in}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

For a generalized chain of $n$ impedances, the voltage across the $k$-th element is:

$$V_k = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Z_i}$$

### Capacitor Series — Important Special Case

For two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ in series, the equivalent capacitance simplifies to:

$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

This is the **product-over-sum** rule — analogous to parallel resistors.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sensor Signal Conditioning**: Resistive voltage dividers are used in potentiometric sensors, Wheatstone bridges, and ADC front-ends.
- **Filter Design**: Series RC, RL, and RLC circuits form the building blocks of first-order and second-order filters (low-pass, high-pass).
- **Power Distribution**: Series impedance in transmission lines is reduced using network theorems for fault analysis.
- **Control Systems**: Transfer functions of cascaded (series) blocks are multiplied, requiring impedance reduction at each stage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Equivalent Resistance of $n$ Resistors in Series

**Starting Premise:** Consider $n$ resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n$ connected in series, with a current $I$ flowing through them.

**Step 1 — Apply Ohm's Law to each resistor:**
The voltage drop across each resistor is $V_k = I \cdot R_k$.

$$V_1 = I R_1, \quad V_2 = I R_2, \quad V_3 = I R_3, \quad \ldots, \quad V_n = I R_n$$

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop:**

$$V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \cdots + V_n$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Ohm's law expressions:**

$$V_{total} = I R_1 + I R_2 + I R_3 + \cdots + I R_n$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the common current $I$:**

$$V_{total} = I \cdot (R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_n)$$

**Step 5 — Define the equivalent resistance $R_{eq}$ such that $V_{total} = I \cdot R_{eq}$:**

$$\boxed{R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The equivalent resistance is **always greater** than the largest individual resistance in the series chain. This is a useful sanity check during exams.

---

## Derivation 2: Equivalent Capacitance of $n$ Capacitors in Series

**Starting Premise:** Consider $n$ capacitors $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ in series with the same charge $Q$ stored on each (since series capacitors carry the same current, hence accumulate the same charge over time).

**Step 1 — Apply the capacitor voltage relation $V = Q/C$ to each capacitor:**

$$V_1 = \frac{Q}{C_1}, \quad V_2 = \frac{Q}{C_2}, \quad \ldots, \quad V_n = \frac{Q}{C_n}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KVL across the series chain:**

$$V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the voltage-charge relations:**

$$V_{total} = \frac{Q}{C_1} + \frac{Q}{C_2} + \cdots + \frac{Q}{C_n}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the common charge $Q$:**

$$V_{total} = Q \cdot \left( \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{C_n} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Define equivalent capacitance $C_{eq}$ with $V_{total} = Q/C_{eq}$:**

$$\frac{1}{C_{eq}} = \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{C_n}$$

$$\boxed{C_{eq} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{C_1} + \dfrac{1}{C_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{C_n}}}$$

**Special Case — Two Capacitors in Series:**

$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The equivalent capacitance of capacitors in series is **always smaller** than the smallest individual capacitance — opposite to the resistor case.

---

## Derivation 3: Voltage Divider Rule (VDR)

**Starting Premise:** Two impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ are connected in series across a source voltage $V_{in}$. Let $V_{out}$ be the voltage across $Z_2$.

**Step 1 — Total impedance of the series combination:**

$$Z_{total} = Z_1 + Z_2$$

**Step 2 — Current flowing through the series loop (using Ohm's law):**

$$I = \frac{V_{in}}{Z_{total}} = \frac{V_{in}}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage across $Z_2$ (output voltage):**

$$V_{out} = I \cdot Z_2 = \frac{V_{in} \cdot Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage across $Z_1$ (for completeness):**

$$V_{Z_1} = I \cdot Z_1 = \frac{V_{in} \cdot Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning:** The voltage divides in **direct proportion** to the impedance. The larger the impedance, the larger its share of the total voltage. This is the heart of potentiometer action.

---

## Derivation 4: Transfer Function of a Series RC Circuit (Control Systems Context)

**Starting Premise:** A resistor $R$ and capacitor $C$ are connected in series. The input voltage $V_{in}(s)$ is applied across the series combination, and the output voltage $V_{out}(s)$ is taken across the capacitor.

**Step 1 — Convert each element to its s-domain impedance:**
- Resistor: $Z_R = R$
- Capacitor: $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{sC}$

**Step 2 — Compute total series impedance:**

$$Z_{total}(s) = R + \frac{1}{sC} = \frac{sRC + 1}{sC}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the current in the loop:**

$$I(s) = \frac{V_{in}(s)}{Z_{total}(s)} = \frac{V_{in}(s) \cdot sC}{sRC + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute output voltage across the capacitor:**

$$V_{out}(s) = I(s) \cdot Z_C = \frac{V_{in}(s) \cdot sC}{sRC + 1} \cdot \frac{1}{sC}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify to obtain the transfer function:**

$$\boxed{G(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** This is a **first-order low-pass filter** with a time constant $\tau = RC$ and a cutoff frequency $\omega_c = 1/RC$. It appears in nearly every control system and signal processing module.

---

## Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Three resistors $R_1 = 10 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = 20 \, \Omega$, and $R_3 = 30 \, \Omega$ are connected in series across a $120 \, V$ source. Find (a) the equivalent resistance, (b) the circuit current, (c) the voltage across $R_2$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Equivalent Resistance:**

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 10 + 20 + 30 = 60 \, \Omega$$

**(b) Circuit Current:**

$$I = \frac{V}{R_{eq}} = \frac{120}{60} = 2 \, A$$

**(c) Voltage across $R_2$ (using VDR):**

$$V_{R_2} = V \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_{eq}} = 120 \cdot \frac{20}{60} = 40 \, V$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Series Connection — Physical Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Terminal A] --> B[Resistor R1]
    B --> C[Common Node]
    C --> D[Resistor R2]
    D --> E[Common Node]
    E --> F[Resistor R3]
    F --> G[Terminal B]
    G --> H[(Return to Source)]

    style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style G fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    style D fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    style F fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** The current enters Terminal A, passes sequentially through $R_1$, $R_2$, and $R_3$ without any branching, and returns to Terminal B. This is the visual signature of a **series chain**.

---

## Diagram 2: Network Reduction Process — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[START: Identify Network] --> stepB{Is it a Series Chain?}
    stepB -- Yes --> stepC[Apply KVL: V total equals V1 plus V2 plus V3]
    stepB -- No --> stepD[Check for Parallel or Star Delta]

    stepC --> stepE[Apply Ohm's Law: Vk equals I times Zk]
    stepE --> stepF[Factor out the Common Current I]
    stepF --> stepG[Compute Z eq equals sum of Zk]
    stepG --> stepH[Verify by Power Balance]
    stepH --> stepI[Apply Voltage Divider Rule if needed]
    stepI --> stepJ[FINAL: Equivalent Single Element]

    stepD --> stepK[Use Parallel Reduction or Star Delta Transform]
    stepK --> stepJ

    style stepA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B88600,color:#000
    style stepB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
    style stepC fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    style stepE fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    style stepG fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000
    style stepJ fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style stepD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** This flowchart captures the **algorithmic decision process** a student should follow when faced with any network reduction problem. The critical decision point is whether the chain is purely series — only then does the simple additive rule apply.

---

## Diagram 3: Series RC Circuit and Transfer Function Derivation Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Vin in s domain] --> resBlock[Impedance R]
    resBlock --> nodeX[Node X]
    nodeX --> capBlock[Impedance 1 over sC]
    capBlock --> groundNode[Ground]

    nodeX --> outTee[Output Tap]
    outTee --> voutNode[Vout in s domain]

    style inputA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B88600,color:#000
    style resBlock fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    style capBlock fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    style voutNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram maps the **series RC low-pass filter** topology. The output is tapped at the junction between $R$ and $C$, illustrating why the transfer function denominator contains the sum of both impedances.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**State the Voltage Divider Rule for two impedances in series. Mention its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The Voltage Divider Rule (VDR) states that when two impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ are connected in series across a source voltage $V_{in}$, the voltage $V_{out}$ appearing across $Z_2$ is given by:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

**Physical Significance:** VDR explains how the source voltage divides between the series elements in **direct proportion to their impedances**. It is the operating principle of potentiometers, sensor biasing networks, and tapped voltage supplies. In control systems, it is the foundation for deriving transfer functions of series RC and RL circuits.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Exam]

**Why is the equivalent capacitance of capacitors in series always less than the smallest individual capacitance?**

**Model Answer:**
For $n$ capacitors in series, the governing equation is:

$$\frac{1}{C_{eq}} = \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{C_n}$$

Since each reciprocal term $\dfrac{1}{C_k}$ is **positive**, the sum of reciprocals is **greater than any single reciprocal**. Mathematically:

$$\frac{1}{C_{eq}} > \frac{1}{C_{min}} \implies C_{eq} < C_{min}$$

This is because the series capacitors effectively increase the **total dielectric thickness** (distance between the equivalent plates), thereby reducing capacitance since $C = \varepsilon A / d$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE format requires **internal choice** between two questions of equal marks. Both choices must be attempted at the syllabus level.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the equivalent resistance of $n$ resistors connected in series. State two necessary conditions for elements to be in series. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the network shown below, find the equivalent resistance between terminals A and B using series reduction. Also compute the current drawn from a 50 V source connected across A-B.

**Network:** $R_1 = 5 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = 10 \, \Omega$, $R_3 = 15 \, \Omega$, $R_4 = 20 \, \Omega$ — all connected end-to-end in series.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:** *(Write the full derivation as in Derivation 1 of Section 3 of these notes. Key marks are awarded for: stating KVL, applying Ohm's law, factoring out I, and writing the final boxed formula. State the two conditions: same current and single current path.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute equivalent resistance using series reduction:

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 = 5 + 10 + 15 + 20 = 50 \, \Omega$$

Step 2 — Apply Ohm's law to find the source current:

$$I = \frac{V}{R_{eq}} = \frac{50}{50} = 1 \, A$$

Step 3 — Optional verification using voltage drops (KTU examiners love this):

$$V_{R_1} = 5 \, V, \quad V_{R_2} = 10 \, V, \quad V_{R_3} = 15 \, V, \quad V_{R_4} = 20 \, V$$

$$\text{Total} = 5 + 10 + 15 + 20 = 50 \, V \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the transfer function $\dfrac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)}$ of a series RC circuit where the output is taken across the capacitor. Identify the type of filter and its cutoff frequency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Three capacitors $C_1 = 2 \, \mu F$, $C_2 = 4 \, \mu F$, and $C_3 = 6 \, \mu F$ are connected in series across a $100 \, V$ DC source. Find the equivalent capacitance and the voltage across each capacitor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Transfer Function Derivation:** *(Write the full derivation as in Derivation 4 of Section 3. Key marks are for: s-domain impedance conversion, total series impedance, current computation, voltage across capacitor, simplification, and identification of the filter type.)*

The transfer function is:

$$G(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

This is a **first-order low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = 1/RC$ rad/s.

**Part (b) — Capacitor Network Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute equivalent capacitance:

$$\frac{1}{C_{eq}} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{6 + 3 + 2}{12} = \frac{11}{12} \, \mu F^{-1}$$

$$C_{eq} = \frac{12}{11} \approx 1.091 \, \mu F$$

Step 2 — Compute the charge on the series combination (same for all):

$$Q = C_{eq} \cdot V = 1.091 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 109.1 \, \mu C$$

Step 3 — Compute voltage across each capacitor:

$$V_{C_1} = \frac{Q}{C_1} = \frac{109.1}{2} = 54.55 \, V$$

$$V_{C_2} = \frac{Q}{C_2} = \frac{109.1}{4} = 27.27 \, V$$

$$V_{C_3} = \frac{Q}{C_3} = \frac{109.1}{6} = 18.18 \, V$$

Step 4 — Verification (sum should equal 100 V):

$$V_{C_1} + V_{C_2} + V_{C_3} = 54.55 + 27.27 + 18.18 = 100 \, V \quad \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Series Voltage Sources:** When multiple EMF sources are in series, their polarities must be tracked carefully. Use a sign convention (e.g., rise = positive) and apply algebraic summation. Many students simply add magnitudes.
> 2. **Confusing Series vs Parallel Capacitor Formulas:** A frequent blunder is writing $C_{eq} = C_1 + C_2 + \cdots$ for series capacitors. **Remember:** resistors and inductors add directly; capacitors use the **reciprocal sum** rule.
> 3. **Forgetting Mutual Inductance:** If two series inductors are magnetically coupled, the equivalent is $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 \pm 2M$. Forgetting the $\pm 2M$ term can cost 2 full marks.
> 4. **Wrong VDR Application:** VDR is valid **only** for two elements in series (or generalized form for $n$ elements). Applying it across parallel branches is a fatal error.
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $\mu F$, $nF$, and $pF$ in capacitor problems without converting to a common unit is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize This Block**

- [x] **Series Resistors:** $R_{eq} = \sum R_k$ → Equivalent is **greater** than the largest.
- [x] **Series Inductors:** $L_{eq} = \sum L_k$ → Same formula as resistors (no coupling).
- [x] **Series Inductors (Coupled):** $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 \pm 2M$ → Sign depends on aiding/opposing.
- [x] **Series Capacitors:** $\dfrac{1}{C_{eq}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{C_k}$ → Equivalent is **smaller** than the smallest.
- [x] **Series Impedances:** $Z_{eq} = \sum Z_k$ → Use **phasor (complex) addition** in AC.
- [x] **Same Current Rule:** The hallmark of series — current is identical through all elements.
- [x] **Voltage Divider Rule (VDR):** $V_k = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{Z_k}{Z_{total}}$ — direct proportionality.
- [x] **Series Voltage Sources:** Algebraic sum with proper sign tracking.
- [x] **Series Current Sources:** Cannot be combined directly — use source transformation first.
- [x] **Transfer Function of Series RC:** $\dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$ — first-order low-pass filter, $\tau = RC$.
- [x] **Transfer Function of Series RL:** $\dfrac{sL}{R + sL}$ — first-order high-pass filter.
- [x] **Power Check:** $P_{total} = \sum P_k$ must hold for verification.
- [x] **Identification Trick:** If you can draw a single loop with no branches, it is series.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT631 - NETWORK AND LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parallel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Parallel Interconnection of Two-Port Networks

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Parallel Interconnection of Two-Port Networks:**
> Two two-port networks $\mathcal{N}_1$ and $\mathcal{N}_2$ are said to be connected in **parallel** when their respective **input ports are tied together** (same voltage across each input port) and their respective **output ports are tied together** (same voltage across each output port), while the **port currents add up** at each node due to KCL.

Mathematically, for two two-port networks $\mathcal{N}_A$ and $\mathcal{N}_B$ connected in parallel:

$$V_{1} = V_{1A} = V_{1B}$$

$$V_{2} = V_{2A} = V_{2B}$$

$$I_{1} = I_{1A} + I_{1B}$$

$$I_{2} = I_{2A} + I_{2B}$$

This set of equations is the **defining identity of parallel combination**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water-Pipe Analogy:**
> Imagine two water tanks feeding into a common outlet pipe. The **pressure (voltage)** at the outlet is the same for both tanks (they share the outlet), but the **total water flow (current)** at the outlet is the **sum** of the individual flows from each tank. This is *exactly* how parallel electrical networks behave — **same voltage, additive current**.

A simpler example familiar to every B.Tech student — **resistors in parallel**:

$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad G_{eq} = G_1 + G_2$$

where $G$ is the **admittance** (the reciprocal of impedance). The current divides in inverse proportion to the resistances because the voltage is common. Two-port parallel combination generalises this principle to the **frequency domain** using **admittance parameters**.

---

## 1.3 Why "Parallel" → Naturally Uses Y-Parameters?

The defining equations of parallel interconnection directly mirror the **Y-parameter (Short-Circuit Admittance Parameter)** representation of a two-port network:

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Y_{11} & Y_{12} \\ Y_{21} & Y_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Because both currents are expressed as functions of voltages, **Y-matrices simply add** when ports are placed in parallel — analogous to admittances adding when resistors are placed in parallel.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** Whenever a problem says *"two-port networks are connected in parallel"*, the immediate tool is the **Y-matrix**. The combined network's $Y_{matrix} = Y_A + Y_B$ (provided the connection is *valid*).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Rules

A parallel interconnection of two two-port networks obeys these rules:

- **Rule 1 — Voltage Identity:** $V_1 = V_{1A} = V_{1B}$ and $V_2 = V_{2A} = V_{2B}$. The input port voltages are physically the *same node*, so they must be equal.

- **Rule 2 — Current Additivity (KCL):** $I_1 = I_{1A} + I_{1B}$ and $I_2 = I_{2A} + I_{2B}$. By Kirchhoff's Current Law, currents entering the common node from each sub-network sum to give the total port current.

- **Rule 3 — Admittance Addition:** Because the Y-matrix equations are linear in voltages, and the voltages are identical, the resulting Y-matrix of the combined network is the **arithmetic sum**:

$$[Y]_{combined} = [Y]_A + [Y]_B$$

- **Rule 4 — Validity Check (Most Forgotten Step!):** The parallel connection is *valid* (i.e., it does not violate any internal constraints) only if, **with both output ports short-circuited ($V_2 = 0$)**, the **current flowing from input port of $\mathcal{N}_A$ into $\mathcal{N}_B$'s input port is zero**, and vice versa. This is verified using the **Hybrid-h parameter condition**:

$$\Delta h_A = 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \Delta h_B = 0$$

where $\Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21}$.

> [!WARNING]
> If this condition fails, the Y-matrices **cannot be simply added**. The student must derive new port equations from scratch. KTU frequently tests this validity check.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Defining Equation** | **Use in Parallel?** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Y_{11}$ | $\left.\dfrac{I_1}{V_1}\right\vert_{V_2=0}$ | ✅ Directly summable | Siemens (S) |
| $Y_{12}$ | $\left.\dfrac{I_1}{V_2}\right\vert_{V_1=0}$ | ✅ Directly summable | Siemens (S) |
| $Y_{21}$ | $\left.\dfrac{I_2}{V_1}\vert_{V_2=0}\right.$ | ✅ Directly summable | Siemens (S) |
| $Y_{22}$ | $\left.\dfrac{I_2}{V_2}\right\vert_{V_1=0}$ | ✅ Directly summable | Siemens (S) |
| Combined Y-matrix | $[Y]_{eq} = [Y]_A + [Y]_B$ | Master formula for parallel | Siemens (S) |
| Validity condition | $\Delta h_A = \Delta h_B = 0$ | Pre-check before adding Y | Dimensionless |
| Conversion to Z | $[Z] = [Y]^{-1}$ if $det([Y]) \neq 0$ | For post-analysis | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Conversion to T (ABCD) | $A = -\dfrac{Y_{22}}{Y_{21}},\ B = -\dfrac{1}{Y_{21}},\ C = -\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y_{21}},\ D = -\dfrac{Y_{11}}{Y_{21}}$ | For cascade follow-up | Mixed |
| Conversion to h | $h_{11}=\dfrac{1}{Y_{11}},\ h_{12}=-\dfrac{Y_{12}}{Y_{11}},\ h_{21}=\dfrac{Y_{21}}{Y_{11}},\ h_{22}=\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y_{11}}$ | Validity check | Mixed |

> **Where:** $\Delta Y = Y_{11}Y_{22} - Y_{12}Y_{21}$.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

Parallel interconnection is ubiquitous in engineering:

- **Power Systems:** Two generators feeding a common bus-bar — modelled as parallel-connected sources for load-sharing analysis.
- **Filter Design:** Multi-section band-pass filters are realised by paralleling L-sections to broaden the bandwidth.
- **Active Networks:** Operational-amplifier summers and current-sharing circuits use parallel topologies.
- **Control Systems:** When two sub-systems drive the same actuator, their transfer functions combine in parallel as $G_{eq}(s) = G_1(s) + G_2(s)$, with output adders — this is the **parallel control structure**.

The Y-parameter framework is the natural mathematical language of these parallel systems, just as Z-parameters are natural for series connections.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Y-Matrices Add in Parallel

### Step 1 — Start with Y-representation of each sub-network

For network $\mathcal{N}_A$:

$$I_{1A} = Y_{11A} \, V_{1A} + Y_{12A} \, V_{2A}$$

$$I_{2A} = Y_{21A} \, V_{1A} + Y_{22A} \, V_{2A}$$

This can be written compactly as:

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_{1A} \\ I_{2A} \end{bmatrix} = [Y]_A \begin{bmatrix} V_{1A} \\ V_{2A} \end{bmatrix}$$

For network $\mathcal{N}_B$ similarly:

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_{1B} \\ I_{2B} \end{bmatrix} = [Y]_B \begin{bmatrix} V_{1B} \\ V_{2B} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the parallel-connection constraints

From the defining conditions of parallel combination:

$$V_{1A} = V_{1B} = V_1 \quad ; \quad V_{2A} = V_{2B} = V_2$$

This means **the voltage column-vector is identical for both networks**:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_{1A} \\ V_{2A} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} V_{1B} \\ V_{2B} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Apply KCL at each common port

By Kirchhoff's Current Law at the input port node:

$$I_1 = I_{1A} + I_{1B}$$

By Kirchhoff's Current Law at the output port node:

$$I_2 = I_{2A} + I_{2B}$$

Therefore the total port-current vector is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} I_{1A} \\ I_{2A} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} I_{1B} \\ I_{2B} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Y-equations

Substituting Step 1 into Step 3:

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = [Y]_A \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} + [Y]_B \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5 — Factor out the common voltage vector

Because both matrices multiply the *same* voltage vector, the distributive law of matrix algebra gives:

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \left( [Y]_A + [Y]_B \right) \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 6 — Identify the equivalent Y-matrix

Comparing with the standard form $\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = [Y]_{eq} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$:

$$\boxed{\,[Y]_{eq} = [Y]_A + [Y]_B\,}$$

**Q.E.D.** The Y-parameters of two parallel-connected two-port networks add element-wise, provided the connection is valid.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Validity Condition via Hybrid Parameters

A parallel connection is *physically realisable* (no internal loop where current circulates between the two networks through their input leads) **iff** the **hybrid-h determinant is zero** for each network. Here is the proof sketch.

For network $\mathcal{N}_A$ in h-form:

$$V_1 = h_{11A} \, I_1 + h_{12A} \, V_2$$

$$I_2 = h_{21A} \, I_1 + h_{22A} \, V_2$$

When the two networks are paralleled, *both* share the same $V_1$ and $V_2$. Subtract the $V_1$ equations:

$$0 = (h_{11A} - h_{11B}) I_{1A} + (h_{12A} - h_{12B}) V_2$$

For this to hold for *all* valid $I_{1A}$ and $V_2$ without forcing one of the networks to source/sink an internal current, we require the **determinant of the coefficient matrix to vanish**, i.e.:

$$(h_{11A} - h_{11B})(h_{22A} - h_{22B}) - (h_{12A} - h_{12B})(h_{21A} - h_{21B}) = 0$$

This is automatically satisfied when both individual determinants are zero:

$$h_{11A} h_{22A} - h_{12A} h_{21A} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad h_{11B} h_{22B} - h_{12B} h_{21B} = 0$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Illustration

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Network A has $[Y]_A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$ S. Network B has $[Y]_B = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -1 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$ S. Find the equivalent Y-matrix when connected in parallel. Verify validity.

**Solution:**

$$[Y]_{eq} = [Y]_A + [Y]_B = \begin{bmatrix} 4+3 & -2+(-1) \\ -2+(-1) & 5+6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & -3 \\ -3 & 11 \end{bmatrix} \text{ S}$$

$$\Delta Y_{eq} = (7)(11) - (-3)(-3) = 77 - 9 = 68 \neq 0$$

The combined network is **reciprocal and non-degenerate**. To verify validity, convert to h-parameters:

$$h_{11A} = \frac{1}{Y_{11A}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

$$h_{12A} = -\frac{Y_{12A}}{Y_{11A}} = -\frac{-2}{4} = 0.5$$

$$h_{21A} = \frac{Y_{21A}}{Y_{11A}} = \frac{-2}{4} = -0.5$$

$$h_{22A} = \frac{\Delta Y_A}{Y_{11A}} = \frac{(4)(5) - (-2)(-2)}{4} = \frac{20 - 4}{4} = 4$$

$$\Delta h_A = (0.25)(4) - (0.5)(-0.5) = 1 - (-0.25) = 1.25 \neq 0$$

> [!WARNING]
> The validity condition **fails**. Despite $Y$-matrices being well-defined and summable, the *physical parallel connection* of these specific networks would produce circulating currents between the input ports. The student must recognise this in KTU problems — adding Y-matrices blindly is a common trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Parallel Connection of Two-Port Networks — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ExtCirc["External Port 1 Circuit"]
        V1["V1 Source"]
    end

    subgraph NetA["Two-Port Network A"]
        A1["Port 1A"]
        A2["Port 2A"]
        A1 --- A2
    end

    subgraph NetB["Two-Port Network B"]
        B1["Port 1B"]
        B2["Port 2B"]
        B1 --- B2
    end

    subgraph ExtCirc2["External Port 2 Circuit"]
        V2["V2 Source"]
    end

    V1 --> A1
    V1 --> B1
    A2 --> V2
    B2 --> V2

    A1 -. "I1A" .-> A1
    B1 -. "I1B" .-> B1
    A2 -. "I2A" .-> A2
    B2 -. "I2B" .-> B2

    classDef net fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef port fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A1,A2,B1,B2 net
    class V1,V2 port
```

> The diagram shows two networks sharing a **common input node (V1)** and a **common output node (V2)**, while their individual port currents ($I_{1A}, I_{1B}, I_{2A}, I_{2B}$) sum at each node.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start(["Input Voltage Vector V1 V2"]) --> Split{V1 and V2 Common to Both}
    Split --> BlockA["Network A: Y_A Matrix"]
    Split --> BlockB["Network B: Y_B Matrix"]

    BlockA --> CurrA["Current Vector I1A I2A"]
    BlockB --> CurrB["Current Vector I1B I2B"]

    CurrA --> Sum["KCL Addition Node"]
    CurrB --> Sum

    Sum --> TotalI["Total Port Currents I1 I2"]
    TotalI --> Check{"Validity Check det h = 0"}
    Check -->|Valid| Combined["Combined Y_eq = Y_A + Y_B"]
    Check -->|Invalid| Invalid["INVALID Parallel Combination"]
    Combined --> End(["Output Y_eq Matrix"])

    classDef proc fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class BlockA,BlockB,Combined proc
    class Start,End,CurrA,CurrB,TotalI io
    class Split,Check decision
```

> This functional flow chart maps the **logical workflow** for analysing any KTU parallel-interconnection problem: apply voltage identity → compute individual Y-matrices → sum currents via KCL → verify validity → produce equivalent Y-matrix.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEERT631]`

**Q: Two two-port networks are connected in parallel. State the conditions for the validity of this parallel connection.**

**Model Answer:**

The parallel connection of two-port networks is valid if and only if the following conditions are satisfied simultaneously:

1. The two networks must have a **common reference node** (ground) — otherwise port voltages cannot be defined uniquely.

2. The **determinant of the hybrid-h parameter matrix** of *each* individual network must equal zero:

$$\Delta h_A = h_{11A} h_{22A} - h_{12A} h_{21A} = 0$$

$$\Delta h_B = h_{11B} h_{22B} - h_{12B} h_{21B} = 0$$

This ensures that no internal circulating current flows between the input leads of the two networks when the output ports are shorted. When valid, the equivalent Y-matrix is simply $[Y]_{eq} = [Y]_A + [Y]_B$.

**(3 Marks — 1 for stating common ground, 1 each for the two determinant conditions)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEERT631]`

**Q: Why are Y-parameters the natural choice for analysing parallel-connected two-port networks?**

**Model Answer:**

Y-parameters (Short-Circuit Admittance Parameters) express **port currents as linear functions of port voltages**:

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = [Y] \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

In a parallel connection, the defining physical constraints are:
- *Voltage identity:* $V_1$ and $V_2$ are common to both networks.
- *Current additivity (KCL):* $I_1 = I_{1A} + I_{1B}$, $I_2 = I_{2A} + I_{2B}$.

Because the same voltage vector drives both networks and the currents simply add, the matrices add algebraically: $[Y]_{eq} = [Y]_A + [Y]_B$. This direct, linear, and unambiguous addition is unique to Y-parameters — Z-parameters, h-parameters, and ABCD-parameters do not admit such a clean rule for parallel combination.

**(3 Marks — 1 for equation, 1 for physical reasoning, 1 for contrast with other parameters)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PEERT631]`

**Two two-port networks are connected in parallel. The Y-parameters of Network A are $Y_{11A}=5, Y_{12A}=-1, Y_{21A}=-2, Y_{22A}=3$ (in Siemens). The Y-parameters of Network B are $Y_{11B}=2, Y_{12B}=-3, Y_{21B}=-1, Y_{22B}=4$ (in Siemens).**

### (a) Determine the Y-parameters of the combined parallel-connected network. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Given:

$$[Y]_A = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & -1 \\ -2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \text{ S}, \qquad [Y]_B = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ -1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \text{ S}$$

For a valid parallel connection:

$$[Y]_{eq} = [Y]_A + [Y]_B$$

Computing element-wise:

$$Y_{11,eq} = Y_{11A} + Y_{11B} = 5 + 2 = 7 \text{ S}$$

$$Y_{12,eq} = Y_{12A} + Y_{12B} = -1 + (-3) = -4 \text{ S}$$

$$Y_{21,eq} = Y_{21A} + Y_{21B} = -2 + (-1) = -3 \text{ S}$$

$$Y_{22,eq} = Y_{22A} + Y_{22B} = 3 + 4 = 7 \text{ S}$$

$$\boxed{\,[Y]_{eq} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & -4 \\ -3 & 7 \end{bmatrix} \text{ S}\,}$$

**(7 Marks — 2 for stating formula, 4 for element-wise computation, 1 for final boxed answer)**

---

### (b) Verify the validity of the parallel connection using the hybrid-h determinant test. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert Network A's Y-parameters to h-parameters.**

$$h_{11A} = \frac{1}{Y_{11A}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$h_{12A} = -\frac{Y_{12A}}{Y_{11A}} = -\frac{-1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

$$h_{21A} = \frac{Y_{21A}}{Y_{11A}} = \frac{-2}{5} = -0.4 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

$$h_{22A} = \frac{\Delta Y_A}{Y_{11A}} = \frac{(5)(3) - (-1)(-2)}{5} = \frac{15 - 2}{5} = \frac{13}{5} = 2.6 \text{ S}$$

Compute the determinant:

$$\Delta h_A = h_{11A} h_{22A} - h_{12A} h_{21A} = (0.2)(2.6) - (0.2)(-0.4) = 0.52 + 0.08 = 0.6$$

**Step 2: Convert Network B's Y-parameters to h-parameters.**

$$h_{11B} = \frac{1}{Y_{11B}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$h_{12B} = -\frac{Y_{12B}}{Y_{11B}} = -\frac{-3}{2} = 1.5 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

$$h_{21B} = \frac{Y_{21B}}{Y_{11B}} = \frac{-1}{2} = -0.5 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

$$h_{22B} = \frac{\Delta Y_B}{Y_{11B}} = \frac{(2)(4) - (-3)(-1)}{2} = \frac{8 - 3}{2} = 2.5 \text{ S}$$

Compute the determinant:

$$\Delta h_B = h_{11B} h_{22B} - h_{12B} h_{21B} = (0.5)(2.5) - (1.5)(-0.5) = 1.25 + 0.75 = 2.0$$

**Step 3: Validity Conclusion.**

$$\Delta h_A = 0.6 \neq 0 \quad ; \quad \Delta h_B = 2.0 \neq 0$$

Since **neither** $\Delta h_A$ nor $\Delta h_B$ equals zero, the parallel connection **is not physically valid**. The simple addition $[Y]_{eq} = [Y]_A + [Y]_B$ would yield an *incorrect* equivalent network because internal circulating currents would flow between the input ports of the two networks when the output ports are shorted. The correct equivalent must be derived from a fresh port analysis treating the combined four-terminal structure as a single two-port.

**(7 Marks — 3 for Network A's h-parameters and determinant, 3 for Network B's h-parameters and determinant, 1 for the validity conclusion)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PEERT631]`

**A two-port network $\mathcal{N}_1$ has Z-parameters $[Z]_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 4 \\ 4 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$ $\Omega$, and network $\mathcal{N}_2$ has Z-parameters $[Z]_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 2 & 12 \end{bmatrix}$ $\Omega$. The two networks are to be connected in parallel.**

### (a) Determine whether the parallel connection is valid. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert Z-parameters to Y-parameters** (Y is needed for parallel connection).

For Network 1:

$$\Delta Z_1 = (10)(8) - (4)(4) = 80 - 16 = 64 \text{ }\Omega^2$$

$$Y_{11,1} = \frac{Z_{22,1}}{\Delta Z_1} = \frac{8}{64} = 0.125 \text{ S}$$

$$Y_{12,1} = -\frac{Z_{12,1}}{\Delta Z_1} = -\frac{4}{64} = -0.0625 \text{ S}$$

$$Y_{21,1} = -\frac{Z_{21,1}}{\Delta Z_1} = -\frac{4}{64} = -0.0625 \text{ S}$$

$$Y_{22,1} = \frac{Z_{11,1}}{\Delta Z_1} = \frac{10}{64} = 0.15625 \text{ S}$$

For Network 2:

$$\Delta Z_2 = (6)(12) - (2)(2) = 72 - 4 = 68 \text{ }\Omega^2$$

$$Y_{11,2} = \frac{Z_{22,2}}{\Delta Z_2} = \frac{12}{68} = 0.1765 \text{ S}$$

$$Y_{12,2} = -\frac{Z_{12,2}}{\Delta Z_2} = -\frac{2}{68} = -0.0294 \text{ S}$$

$$Y_{21,2} = -\frac{Z_{21,2}}{\Delta Z_2} = -\frac{2}{68} = -0.0294 \text{ S}$$

$$Y_{22,2} = \frac{Z_{11,2}}{\Delta Z_2} = \frac{6}{68} = 0.0882 \text{ S}$$

**Step 2: Compute equivalent Y-parameters (assuming validity).**

$$[Y]_{eq} = [Y]_1 + [Y]_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3015 & -0.0919 \\ -0.0919 & 0.2445 \end{bmatrix} \text{ S}$$

**Step 3: Validity Check via hybrid-h determinant.**

For Network 1:

$$h_{11,1} = \frac{1}{Y_{11,1}} = \frac{1}{0.125} = 8 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$h_{12,1} = -\frac{Y_{12,1}}{Y_{11,1}} = -\frac{-0.0625}{0.125} = 0.5$$

$$h_{21,1} = \frac{Y_{21,1}}{Y_{11,1}} = \frac{-0.0625}{0.125} = -0.5$$

$$h_{22,1} = \frac{\Delta Y_1}{Y_{11,1}} = \frac{(0.125)(0.15625) - (-0.0625)(-0.0625)}{0.125} = \frac{0.01953 - 0.00391}{0.125} = 0.125$$

$$\Delta h_1 = (8)(0.125) - (0.5)(-0.5) = 1 + 0.25 = 1.25 \neq 0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** $\Delta h_1 \neq 0$, so the parallel connection is **NOT valid** for these networks as given. The student must declare the connection invalid and cannot proceed with the addition for a physical implementation.

**(7 Marks — 4 for Y-parameter computation, 2 for h-determinant, 1 for validity conclusion)**

---

### (b) If the validity condition were satisfied, write the equivalent Y-matrix expression and explain how the Z-parameters of the combined network can be obtained. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Expression for the Equivalent Y-matrix.**

If the parallel connection were valid:

$$[Y]_{eq} = [Y]_1 + [Y]_2$$

In expanded form:

$$Y_{11,eq} = Y_{11,1} + Y_{11,2}$$

$$Y_{12,eq} = Y_{12,1} + Y_{12,2}$$

$$Y_{21,eq} = Y_{21,1} + Y_{21,2}$$

$$Y_{22,eq} = Y_{22,1} + Y_{22,2}$$

**Step 2: Obtaining the Combined Z-matrix.**

The Z-matrix of the combined network is the inverse of the combined Y-matrix:

$$[Z]_{eq} = [Y]_{eq}^{-1}$$

Provided $\det([Y]_{eq}) \neq 0$. Using the $2 \times 2$ inversion formula:

$$[Z]_{eq} = \frac{1}{\Delta Y_{eq}} \begin{bmatrix} Y_{22,eq} & -Y_{12,eq} \\ -Y_{21,eq} & Y_{11,eq} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\Delta Y_{eq} = Y_{11,eq} Y_{22,eq} - Y_{12,eq} Y_{21,eq}$.

**Step 3: Physical Interpretation.**

The Z-parameters represent the open-circuit behaviour of the equivalent two-port. Hence, once the equivalent Y-matrix is found, inversion yields the open-circuit impedance view of the same combined network. This is often useful for follow-up problems where the examiner asks for the open-circuit input/output impedances (e.g., $Z_{in}$ with port 2 open), which are read directly off the Z-matrix as $Z_{in} = Z_{11,eq}$.

**(7 Marks — 2 for writing the addition rule, 2 for inversion formula, 1 for determinant condition, 2 for physical interpretation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Validity Check (–3 marks typical):** Many students directly write $[Y]_{eq} = [Y]_A + [Y]_B$ without verifying $\Delta h = 0$. Examiners *expect* the validity condition to be checked first.
> 2. **Using Z-matrices for Parallel (–2 marks):** Z-matrices **do not** add in parallel — only Y-matrices do. Mixing up series/parallel parameter rules is the most common error.
> 3. **Sign Error in Conversion Formulas (–1 to 2 marks):** The signs in $h_{12} = -Y_{12}/Y_{11}$ and $h_{21} = Y_{21}/Y_{11}$ are frequently flipped.
> 4. **Units Omission (–1 mark):** Always quote admittances in **Siemens (S)** and impedances in **Ohms ($\Omega$)** in KTU answer sheets.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Parallel Interconnection of Two-Port Networks**

- ✅ Parallel combination **shares voltages**: $V_1 = V_{1A} = V_{1B}$, $V_2 = V_{2A} = V_{2B}$.
- ✅ Parallel combination **adds currents (KCL)**: $I_1 = I_{1A} + I_{1B}$, $I_2 = I_{2A} + I_{2B}$.
- ✅ The **equivalent Y-matrix** is the **element-wise sum**: $[Y]_{eq} = [Y]_A + [Y]_B$.
- ✅ **Y-parameters are the natural tool** because they express $I$ as a function of $V$, mirroring the parallel constraints.
- ✅ **Validity Condition:** Both networks must have **zero hybrid-h determinant** ($\Delta h_A = \Delta h_B = 0$) for the addition to be physically correct.
- ✅ Convert Z → Y using $[Y] = [Z]^{-1}$ before paralleling if data is given in Z-form.
- ✅ After combining Y, convert back to other parameters ($Z$, $T$, $h$) as required using standard formulas.
- ✅ Common exam traps: forgetting validity, wrong parameter type for the connection, sign errors, units.
- ✅ **Engineering uses:** generator paralleling, filter design, op-amp summers, parallel control blocks $G_{eq}(s) = G_1(s) + G_2(s)$.
- ✅ Mermaid diagrams: top node for $V_1$, bottom node for $V_2$, two parallel two-port boxes between them, with currents adding via KCL summation block.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series-Parallel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT631 - NETWORK AND LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Series-Parallel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Network and Linear Control Systems**, the **Series-Parallel combination** refers to the systematic method of reducing complex electrical networks composed of impedances ($R$, $L$, $C$, and dependent sources) into a single equivalent impedance or transfer function by applying the rules of series and parallel interconnections. In the s-domain, an impedance $Z(s)$ is the ratio of voltage to current phasor, and combinations follow algebraic laws analogous to resistors but operate on complex-frequency variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the s-domain, the impedance of an inductor is $Z_L(s) = sL$ and a capacitor is $Z_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC}$. The Series-Parallel reduction is the **first step** before deriving the transfer function $T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ of any electrical network.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a network of water pipes connected to a water tank. The **series** combination is like pipes joined end-to-end — the same water (current) flows through each pipe, and the total resistance to flow (impedance) adds up directly. The **parallel** combination is like multiple pipes connected between the same two points — the water (current) splits among them, but the pressure drop (voltage) across all pipes is identical.

- **Series**: Same current flows; voltages add up.
- **Parallel**: Same voltage appears; currents add up.
