> **Engineering Utility:** These six basic components appear in **every** engineering discipline — DC motor speed control in electrical, cruise control in automotive, autopilot in aerospace, pH control in chemical plants, blood-glucose regulation in biomedical, and HVAC in building automation. Mastering the block diagram is the gateway to the rest of the course.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function with Unity Feedback

**Problem Setup:** Given a forward path transfer function $G(s)$ and a feedback path transfer function $H(s)$, derive the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs and outputs at each block.**

Let the input to the plant be $U(s)$, the feedback signal be $B(s)$, and the error be $E(s)$.

**Step 2 — Write the equation for the error detector (summing junction).**

The error detector subtracts feedback from reference:

$$ E(s) = R(s) - B(s) $$

**Step 3 — Write the equation for the forward path.**

The forward path $G(s)$ multiplies the error by the open-loop gain to produce the controlled output:

$$ Y(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s) $$

**Step 4 — Write the equation for the feedback path.**

The feedback element $H(s)$ converts the output $Y(s)$ into a feedback signal $B(s)$:

$$ B(s) = H(s) \cdot Y(s) $$

**Step 5 — Substitute (4) into (2) to eliminate $B(s)$.**

$$ E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot Y(s) $$

**Step 6 — Substitute (5) into (3) to eliminate $E(s)$.**

$$ Y(s) = G(s) \cdot \big[R(s) - H(s) \cdot Y(s)\big] $$

**Step 7 — Expand and collect $Y(s)$ terms on one side.**

$$ Y(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot Y(s) $$

$$ Y(s) + G(s) H(s) \cdot Y(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) $$

$$ Y(s) \big[1 + G(s) H(s)\big] = G(s) \cdot R(s) $$

**Step 8 — Isolate the ratio $Y(s)/R(s)$.**

$$ T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Unity Feedback ($H(s) = 1$):** The formula simplifies to $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$. This is the most common form encountered in KTU problems. Always check whether $H(s)$ is given explicitly or whether unity feedback is assumed.

### 3.2 Derivation: Why Feedback Reduces Sensitivity to Plant Variations

**Step 1 — Compute the sensitivity of $T$ with respect to $G$ for a closed-loop system.**

$$ \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{\partial}{\partial G}\left[\frac{G}{1+GH}\right] $$

$$ \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{(1+GH) - G \cdot H}{(1+GH)^2} = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} $$

**Step 2 — Form the relative (logarithmic) sensitivity.**

$$ S^T_G = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} \cdot \frac{G}{T} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values of $T$ and $\partial T / \partial G$.**

$$ S^T_G = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} \cdot \frac{G}{G/(1+GH)} $$

$$ S^T_G = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} \cdot (1+GH) $$

$$ S^T_G = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)} $$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

If $\vert G(s) H(s) \vert \gg 1$ (i.e., high loop gain at the operating frequency), then:

$$ S^T_G \approx \frac{1}{G(s) H(s)} \approx 0 $$

This means a large percentage change in $G$ produces a much smaller percentage change in $T$. **Feedback makes the system robust.** For an open-loop system, $S^T_G = 1$ — a 10% drift in $G$ causes a 10% drift in $T$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component vs. Property

| Property | Reference Input $R(s)$ | Error Detector | Controller $G_c$ | Actuator $G_a$ | Plant $G_p$ | Sensor $H$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Symbolic block** | $R(s)$ | $\sum$ | $G_c(s)$ | $G_a(s)$ | $G_p(s)$ | $H(s)$ |
| **Power level** | Low | Low | Low | **High** | High | Low |
| **Typical dynamics** | Step / Ramp | None (algebraic) | P, I, D, PID | 1st/2nd order | 1st/2nd order | 1st order + noise |
| **Failure mode** | Wrong set-point | Sign error | Tuning error | Saturation | Nonlinearity | Noise / drift |
| **Example** | Potentiometer | Op-amp subtractor | Microcontroller | DC motor | Robotic arm | Tachogenerator |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Closed-Loop Control System

The block diagram is the **single most important visual aid** for Module 1. The KTU board examiner expects every student to be able to draw and label this diagram from memory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["R(s) Reference Input"] --> SUM1{"Summing Junction e(t)=r-b"}
    SUM1 --> E["E(s) Error Signal"]
    E --> GC["Gc(s) Controller Compensator"]
    GC --> GA["Ga(s) Actuator Power Amplifier"]
    GA --> GP["Gp(s) Plant Process"]
    GP --> Y["Y(s) Controlled Output"]
    Y --> H["H(s) Feedback Sensor Transducer"]
    H --> FB["B(s) Feedback Signal"]
    FB --> SUM1
    Y -. Disturbance D(s) .-> GP
    Y -. Noise N(s) .-> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrows clockwise. The error is the difference $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$. The forward path cascades $G_c$, $G_a$, and $G_p$. The feedback path passes through $H(s)$. Disturbances $D(s)$ enter the plant, and noise $N(s)$ enters the sensor — both corrupt the ideal response.

### 4.2 Block Diagram of an Open-Loop Control System (Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["R(s) Reference Input"] --> GC["Gc(s) Controller"]
    GC --> GA["Ga(s) Actuator"]
    GA --> GP["Gp(s) Plant"]
    GP --> Y["Y(s) Output"]
    Y -. No feedback .-> X["No Measurement Loop"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the broken feedback path.** In an open-loop system, the output $Y(s)$ is **not** measured and **not** compared with the input. There is no error signal, no error detector, and no feedback sensor. The system is "blind" to disturbances.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comparison of Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClosedLoop["Closed-Loop Architecture FB ON"]
        direction LR
        A1[R] --> B1[SUM]
        B1 --> C1[Controller]
        C1 --> D1[Actuator]
        D1 --> E1[Plant]
        E1 --> F1[Output]
        E1 --> G1[Sensor]
        G1 --> B1
    end
    subgraph OpenLoop["Open-Loop Architecture FB OFF"]
        direction LR
        A2[R] --> C2[Controller]
        C2 --> D2[Actuator]
        D2 --> E2[Plant]
        E2 --> F2[Output]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw the block diagram of a closed-loop control system and label all blocks," examiners look for the **summing junction with + and − signs**, the **forward path cascade**, and the **feedback path returning to the negative input**. Missing the negative sign at the summing junction is the most common cause of mark loss in this question.

### 4.4 Canonical Reduction Steps (Block Diagram Reduction)

Many KTU problems require simplifying a complex block diagram into a single transfer function. The standard reduction rules are:

| # | Rule | Original Form | Reduced Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cascade (series) | $G_1 \to G_2$ | $G_1 G_2$ |
| 2 | Parallel | $G_1 \parallel G_2$ | $G_1 \pm G_2$ |
| 3 | Feedback loop | $G$ with $H$ in FB | $\dfrac{G}{1 \pm GH}$ |
| 4 | Moving a take-off point past a block | Shift, then adjust | Multiply/Divide by $G$ |
| 5 | Moving a summing junction past a block | Shift, then adjust | Multiply/Divide by $G$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention used in all KTU papers:** A **+** sign inside the FB formula corresponds to **negative feedback** (because the error is $R - B$). A **−** sign corresponds to **positive feedback** (regenerative). Be careful — this is a frequent sign-convention trap.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define a control system. With a suitable example, distinguish between an open-loop and a closed-loop control system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribution shown):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A control system is an interconnection of components arranged to command, direct, or regulate itself or another system to achieve a prescribed output from a given input, while rejecting disturbances.

**Open-loop (1 mark):**
An open-loop control system has no feedback path. The control action is independent of the actual output. **Example:** A washing machine running on a preset timer — the machine does not measure the cleanliness of the clothes.

**Closed-loop (1 mark):**
A closed-loop control system has a feedback path. The error between the desired and actual output continuously drives the controller. **Example:** An automatic washing machine with a turbidity sensor that measures dirt level and adjusts wash time accordingly.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**List the basic components of a closed-loop control system. Briefly explain the role of any two components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribution shown):**

**List of components (1 mark):**
The six basic components are:
1. Reference input element
2. Error detector (summing junction)
3. Controller
4. Actuator (final control element)
5. Plant (controlled system)
6. Feedback sensor (transducer)

**Explanation of any two components (2 marks — 1 mark each):**

**Error Detector:** Computes the difference between the reference input $r(t)$ and the feedback signal $b(t)$ to produce the error $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$. It is typically implemented using an operational amplifier in subtractor configuration.

**Actuator:** Receives the low-power control signal from the controller and converts it into a high-power physical input (force, torque, voltage) suitable to drive the plant. **Examples:** DC motor, hydraulic cylinder, pneumatic valve. The actuator is the energy-amplification stage of the loop.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Attempting:**
> 1. **Sign Error (–2 marks):** Writing the closed-loop TF as $\dfrac{G}{1 - GH}$ instead of $\dfrac{G}{1 + GH}$ for negative feedback. The sign inside the denominator is determined by the feedback sign, not by inspection.
> 2. **Forgetting Disturbance/Noise Paths (–1 mark):** When asked to "draw the complete block diagram," examiners expect disturbance $D(s)$ entering the plant and noise $N(s)$ entering the sensor. Omitting these loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Missing the Negative Sign at the Summing Junction (–2 marks):** Drawing a "+" at the feedback input to the summing junction instead of "−". This is the single most common mark-loss in KTU paper-drawing questions.
> 4. **Confusing the Plant with the Controller (–1 mark):** The **plant** is the system being controlled (e.g., a motor); the **controller** is the element that decides the control law (e.g., a PID chip). Mixing these up is a terminology error.
> 5. **Omitting Units (–1 mark):** When giving numerical values like $K_p = 5$ or $\omega_n = 10$ rad/s, always mention the unit. KTU examiners explicitly award ½ to 1 mark for units in numerical answers.

---

#### Part B — Choice 1 (Question A — 14 Marks)

**Q. A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 1 Full Question]**

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$ of a negative-feedback control system with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$. Hence state the transfer function for the special case of unity feedback.

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the basic components of a closed-loop control system. State the function of each component in one sentence.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Let the error detector output be $E(s)$, the controller output be $U(s)$, the plant input be the same $U(s)$, and the feedback signal be $B(s)$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The error detector equation:
$$ E(s) = R(s) - B(s) $$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The forward path output (the plant output):
$$ Y(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s) $$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The feedback path output:
$$ B(s) = H(s) \cdot Y(s) $$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute (4) into (2):
$$ E(s) = R(s) - H(s) Y(s) $$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substitute into (3) and rearrange:
$$ Y(s) = G(s) \big[R(s) - H(s) Y(s)\big] $$

$$ Y(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) Y(s) $$

$$ Y(s) \big[1 + G(s) H(s)\big] = G(s) R(s) $$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final result and unity-feedback case:
$$ T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} $$

For unity feedback $H(s) = 1$:

$$ T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} $$

**Part (b) — Block Diagram and Component Description (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the standard closed-loop block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes, with:
- $R(s)$ as input
- Summing junction (with $+$ for $R$ and $-$ for $B$)
- Forward path: $G_c \to G_a \to G_p$
- Output $Y(s)$
- Feedback path $H(s)$ returning to summing junction
- Disturbance $D(s)$ entering the plant
- Noise $N(s)$ entering the sensor

**Component descriptions (4 marks — 1 mark for each, pick any 4):**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reference input $R(s)$** | Provides the desired value (set point) the output should track. |
| 2 | **Error detector** | Computes $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$, the deviation of output from desired. |
| 3 | **Controller $G_c$** | Applies the control law (P/PI/PD/PID) to the error to produce a control signal. |
| 4 | **Actuator $G_a$** | Amplifies the low-power control signal to a high-power physical input for the plant. |
| 5 | **Plant $G_p$** | The physical process being controlled (e.g., motor, furnace, robot arm). |
| 6 | **Sensor $H(s)$** | Measures actual output $y(t)$ and converts it to a comparable feedback signal. |

---

#### Part B — Choice 2 (Question B — 14 Marks)

**Q. B [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 1 Alternative]**

**(a)** Define **sensitivity** of a control system. Derive the sensitivity of the closed-loop transfer function $T(s)$ with respect to the forward-path transfer function $G(s)$ and show that feedback reduces sensitivity when loop gain is large.

**(b)** Compare open-loop and closed-loop control systems on the basis of (i) accuracy, (ii) disturbance rejection, (iii) stability, (iv) cost, and (v) bandwidth.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Sensitivity Definition and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Sensitivity $S^T_G$ is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in the closed-loop transfer function $T$ to the fractional change in the open-loop transfer function $G$:

$$ S^T_G = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} $$

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the closed-loop TF and the partial derivative setup:
$$ T(s) = \frac{G}{1 + GH} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2} $$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Form the sensitivity expression:
$$ S^T_G = \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} \cdot \frac{G}{T} = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} \cdot \frac{G(1+GH)}{G} $$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Simplify:
$$ S^T_G = \frac{1}{1 + GH} $$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** For open-loop system, $T = G$ directly, so $S = 1$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Interpretation: if $\vert GH \vert \gg 1$, then $S^T_G \approx \dfrac{1}{GH} \ll 1$. Hence a large percentage change in $G$ produces a much smaller percentage change in $T$, proving that **feedback reduces sensitivity**.

**Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks — distribute 1 mark per criterion + 2 marks for table presentation):**

| # | Property | Open Loop | Closed Loop | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Accuracy** | Poor (depends on calibration) | High (error-driven) | 1 |
| 2 | **Disturbance rejection** | None (no feedback) | Excellent | 1 |
| 3 | **Stability** | Always stable if plant stable | Can become unstable | 1 |
| 4 | **Cost & complexity** | Low | High (sensors + controller) | 1 |
| 5 | **Bandwidth** | Wide | Limited by feedback dynamics | 1 |
| 6 | **Sensitivity to plant variation** | High ($S = 1$) | Low ($S = 1/(1+GH)$) | 1 |
| 7 | **Examples** | Traffic light, bread toaster | Cruise control, autopilot | 1 |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A control system regulates a plant's output to match a reference input while rejecting disturbances.
- **Two types:** Open-loop (no feedback) and closed-loop (with feedback).
- **Six basic components:** Reference input, error detector, controller, actuator, plant, sensor.
- **Most important formula:** Closed-loop TF $\,T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\,$ — memorize this cold; it appears in almost every KTU module.
- **Unity feedback simplification:** $H(s) = 1 \Rightarrow T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$.
- **Summing junction sign:** Always check the sign — a "−" in the diagram means "−" in the denominator.
- **Sensitivity result:** Closed-loop sensitivity $S^T_G = \dfrac{1}{1 + GH}$, vs. open-loop $S = 1$. High loop gain ⇒ low sensitivity ⇒ robust system.
- **Steady-state error benchmarks:** For a Type-0 system tracking a step: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$. For a Type-1 system tracking a ramp: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$.
- **Disturbance vs. Noise:** $D(s)$ enters the plant; $N(s)$ enters the sensor. Always include both in a "complete" block diagram.
- **Units to remember:** $\omega_n$ in **rad/s**, time in **seconds**, error in the **same unit as the input**, damping ratio $\zeta$ is dimensionless.
- **Block diagram reduction rules:** Cascade ⇒ multiply; parallel ⇒ add/subtract; feedback loop ⇒ $\dfrac{G}{1 \pm GH}$.
- **Common sign convention trap:** A "+" sign in the loop formula means **negative feedback** (because we computed $E = R - B$). Re-derive from scratch if in doubt.
- **Open-loop is faster but blind; closed-loop is accurate but may oscillate if poorly tuned.** Trade-off awareness earns KTU descriptive-answer marks.
- **Real-world engineering mapping:** DC motor speed → actuator; tachogenerator → sensor; PID chip → controller; shaft speed → output; potentiometer → reference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Control Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT631 - NETWORK AND LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Control Systems

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Control System

A **Control System** is a carefully engineered interconnection of physical components arranged in a deliberate configuration such that the output variable is forced to follow a prescribed reference value, dictated by the input, with a desired degree of accuracy, speed, and stability. Mathematically, it is a mapping from an input signal $r(t)$ to an output signal $c(t)$ through a dynamic operator $\mathcal{H}$ such that:

$$
c(t) = \mathcal{H}\{r(t)\}
$$

The discipline that studies, analyses, and designs such systems is formally called **Control Systems Engineering**, and it occupies a foundational position in modern electrical, mechanical, aerospace, chemical, biomedical, and robotics engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A control system consists of subsystems and processes (or plants) assembled for the purpose of obtaining a desired output with desired performance specifications from a given input.

## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Classifications

For the KTU 2024 Scheme module **PEERT631 – Module 1**, every control system is first classified into one of two universal categories:

| **S.No** | **Primary Classification** | **Feedback Path Present?** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Open-Loop Control System** | **No** |
| 2 | **Closed-Loop Control System** | **Yes** |

This single binary distinction — the presence or absence of a feedback path — governs nearly every analytical tool, design philosophy, and performance trade-off that follows in the rest of the course.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Two Drivers

Imagine you are teaching a friend to drive a car down a straight highway at exactly **60 km/h**:

- **Open-Loop (Blind Driver):** Your friend presses the accelerator pedal to a fixed position based on a *guess* about the slope of the road, the wind, and the car load. They never look at the speedometer. If a hill appears, the car slows down and nothing corrects it. The system has no "knowledge" of the actual outcome.
- **Closed-Loop (Smart Driver):** Your friend constantly watches the speedometer. If the speed drops to **55 km/h**, they press harder; if it rises to **65 km/h**, they ease off. The driver's eyes are the **sensor**, the brain is the **controller**, and the speedometer reading is the **feedback signal**.

> [!NOTE]
> The closed-loop system measures the actual output and *closes the loop* by feeding that measurement back to compare it with the desired reference. This is why closed-loop systems are also called **Feedback Control Systems**.

## 1.4 Extended Hierarchy of Classification

Beyond the binary open/closed split, control systems are also classified along several orthogonal axes. A KTU student is expected to recognize all of them:

$$
\text{Control Systems} \begin{cases}
\text{By Structure} \begin{cases} \text{Open-Loop} \\ \text{Closed-Loop (Feedback)} \end{cases} \\
\text{By Linearity} \begin{cases} \text{Linear} \\ \text{Non-Linear} \end{cases} \\
\text{By Time Nature} \begin{cases} \text{Time-Invariant (LTI)} \\ \text{Time-Varying} \end{cases} \\
\text{By Signal Type} \begin{cases} \text{Continuous-Time} \\ \text{Discrete-Time} \end{cases} \\
\text{By I/O Count} \begin{cases} \text{SISO (Single-Input, Single-Output)} \\ \text{MIMO (Multi-Input, Multi-Output)} \end{cases} \\
\text{By Causality} \begin{cases} \text{Causal} \\ \text{Non-Causal} \end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$

For this Module 1 study sheet, we will provide a rigorous treatment of **Structure-based classification** (Open-Loop and Closed-Loop) since it is the most heavily tested topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Open-Loop Control System — Formal Treatment

An **Open-Loop Control System** is one in which the control action is entirely independent of the output. The signal flow proceeds strictly in **one direction** — from input to output — with **no measurement, comparison, or correction loop**.

### 2.1.1 Structural Composition

The canonical block diagram of an open-loop system contains exactly two cascaded blocks:

$$
\boxed{r(t) \;\longrightarrow\; \boxed{\text{Controller}} \;\longrightarrow\; \boxed{\text{Plant / Process}} \;\longrightarrow\; c(t)}
$$

| **Block** | **Function** | **Real-World Examples** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Controller** | Generates the actuating signal $u(t)$ from the reference $r(t)$ | Bread toaster timer, washing machine dial |
| **Plant / Process** | The physical device whose output we wish to control | Toaster heating coil, washing machine drum motor |

### 2.1.2 Mathematical Representation

The overall transfer function of an open-loop system is the **product** of the transfer functions of all forward-path blocks:

$$
G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)
$$

where $G_c(s)$ is the controller transfer function and $G_p(s)$ is the plant transfer function.

The input-output relationship is therefore:

$$
C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)
$$

### 2.1.3 Detailed Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. The **reference input** $r(t)$ is applied to the controller.
2. The controller converts this reference into a physical **actuating signal** $u(t)$.
3. The actuating signal drives the **plant**, which is the device producing the desired physical output $c(t)$.
4. The output is produced **without any measurement** of whether it actually equals the desired value.
5. **External disturbances** $D(s)$ (load changes, temperature drift, noise) enter the plant and directly corrupt $c(t)$.
6. Since there is **no feedback**, the controller cannot compensate, and the error becomes permanent.

### 2.1.4 Advantages and Disadvantages of Open-Loop Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners frequently test the pros and cons in **Part A (3-mark)** questions.

**Advantages:**

- **Simple in construction and design** — fewer components, lower cost.
- **Economically cheaper** for mass production (e.g., electric iron, toaster).
- **No stability problems** — there is no feedback loop to become unstable.
- **Easier to maintain** — fewer sensors and signal conditioning circuits.
- **Useful when the output is hard or expensive to measure** (e.g., a chemical batch reactor where continuous sensing is impractical).

**Disadvantages:**

- **Inaccurate and unreliable** — accuracy depends entirely on careful pre-calibration.
- **No disturbance rejection** — any unmodelled load change permanently affects output.
- **No correction for parameter drift** due to ageing, temperature, or wear.
- **Output is highly sensitive** to changes in the plant transfer function.
- **Bandwidth is limited** because there is no error-driven correction.

## 2.2 Closed-Loop Control System — Formal Treatment

A **Closed-Loop Control System** (also called a **Feedback Control System**) is one in which the output is continuously measured, compared with the desired reference, and the resulting **error signal** is used to drive the controller so as to reduce the error toward zero.

### 2.2.1 Structural Composition

The canonical closed-loop block diagram has five essential elements:

$$
\boxed{r(t) \;\rightarrow\; \bigotimes \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{G_c(s)} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{G_p(s)} \;\rightarrow\; c(t)}
$$
$$
\hspace{2.5cm} \uparrow \hspace{3.8cm} \downarrow
$$
$$
\hspace{2.0cm} \leftarrow \boxed{H(s)} \;\leftarrow
$$

| **Element** | **Symbol** | **Function** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reference Input** | $r(t)$ | The desired value of the output |
| **Comparator / Summing Junction** | $\otimes$ | Computes $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$ |
| **Controller** | $G_c(s)$ | Processes the error |
| **Plant / Process** | $G_p(s)$ | Produces the physical output |
| **Feedback Element / Sensor** | $H(s)$ | Measures the output and feeds it back |

### 2.2.2 Mathematical Representation

The **error signal** in the Laplace domain is:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - B(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)
$$

The forward-path output is:

$$
C(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot E(s)
$$

Substituting and solving for the **closed-loop transfer function**:

$$
\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \quad \text{where } G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)
$$

This is the single most important formula in the entire module and must be memorized verbatim.

### 2.2.3 Detailed Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. A **reference signal** $r(t)$ representing the desired output is applied to the system.
2. A **sensor** continuously measures the actual output $c(t)$ and converts it into a feedback signal $b(t) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$.
3. The **summing junction** computes the **error** $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$.
4. The **controller** receives $e(t)$ and produces an actuating signal $u(t)$.
5. The **plant** receives $u(t)$ and produces $c(t)$, attempting to drive $e(t)$ toward zero.
6. The loop continuously repeats this cycle at a rate determined by the system dynamics and clock.

### 2.2.4 Advantages and Disadvantages of Closed-Loop Systems

**Advantages:**

- **High accuracy** — feedback corrects errors automatically.
- **Excellent disturbance rejection** — the loop compensates for unknown disturbances.
- **Reduced sensitivity** to plant parameter variations.
- **Improved bandwidth and transient response** through proper compensation.
- **Self-correcting** in the presence of non-linearities and drift.

**Disadvantages:**

- **Complex and expensive** due to sensors, wiring, and signal conditioning.
- **Design is mathematically rigorous** and requires stability analysis (covered in later modules).
- **Risk of instability** — the feedback that provides correction can also cause oscillation if not designed carefully.
- **More maintenance** due to the larger number of components.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Examples

| **Application** | **Open-Loop** | **Closed-Loop** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Domestic** | Electric iron, bread toaster, fan regulator | Refrigerator thermostat, washing machine with load sensor |
| **Industrial** | Conveyor belt at fixed speed, traffic signal timer | CNC machine tool position control, robotic arm |
| **Automotive** | Simple wiper with fixed speed interval | Cruise control, anti-lock braking system (ABS) |
| **Aerospace** | Pre-programmed parachute release | Autopilot, missile guidance, quadcopter drone |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** you will need to solve Part-A and Part-B questions on this topic. Memorize the boxed entries with absolute precision.

| **Sl. No.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Condition / Notes** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Open-loop input-output relation | $C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$ | No feedback path exists |
| 2 | Closed-loop transfer function | $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Negative feedback assumed |
| 3 | Error signal (Laplace) | $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ | $B(s) = H(s)C(s)$ |
| 4 | Forward-path gain | $G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$ | Cascade of all forward blocks |
| 5 | Loop gain | $L(s) = G(s) \cdot H(s)$ | Critical for stability analysis |
| 6 | Return difference | $1 + G(s)H(s)$ | Denominator of closed-loop TF |
| 7 | Feedback TF (unity case) | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ | When $H(s) = 1$ |
| 8 | Sensitivity of $T$ to $G$ | $S_G^T = \dfrac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Feedback reduces sensitivity by factor $1 + GH$ |
| 9 | Disturbance rejection ratio | $\dfrac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \dfrac{G_p(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | $D(s)$ enters at plant input |
| 10 | Negative feedback sign convention | Subtraction at summing junction | $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$ |
| 11 | Positive feedback (rarely used) | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$ | Unstable in most practical cases |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical rule for table display:** All set-theoretic and absolute-value notation in handwritten exams uses vertical bars. In this typed table, vertical bars inside table cells would break the markdown, so they have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` as a notation-preserving alternative. In your exam, write `\vert x \vert` as $\vert x \vert$ on paper.

## 2.5 Sensitivity Analysis — Why Feedback Reduces Parameter Variation

Suppose the plant gain $G$ changes by a small amount $\Delta G$. The resulting change in the closed-loop gain $T$ is given by:

$$
\Delta T = \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} \Delta G = \frac{1}{\left(1 + GH\right)^{2}} \Delta G
$$

Dividing both sides by $T$:

$$
\frac{\Delta T}{T} = \frac{1}{1 + GH} \cdot \frac{\Delta G}{G}
$$

Thus, the **relative sensitivity** of the closed-loop system to plant variations is reduced by the factor $\dfrac{1}{1 + GH}$. This is precisely why high-performance systems (e.g., spacecraft attitude control, atomic force microscopy) use closed-loop architecture — the open-loop gain could drift by 50%, but the closed-loop output would only drift by less than 1%.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function with Negative Feedback

This is the single most important derivation in Module 1. It will appear in almost every Part-B question.

### **Given:**

A unity-negative-feedback block diagram with forward-path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback-path transfer function $H(s)$. The reference is $R(s)$, the output is $C(s)$, the error is $E(s)$, and the feedback signal is $B(s) = H(s) C(s)$.

### **Step 1 — Write the output equation in terms of error.**

The forward path multiplies the error by $G(s)$:

$$
C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)
$$

**Explanation:** The plant accepts the controller output (which equals the error in this canonical case) and produces $C(s)$.

### **Step 2 — Write the error equation from the summing junction.**

The summing junction subtracts the feedback signal from the reference:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - B(s)
$$

**Explanation:** The comparator enforces the negative-feedback convention.

### **Step 3 — Substitute the feedback signal.**

By definition of the feedback path:

$$
B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)
$$

So:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)
$$

**Explanation:** The measured output is scaled by the sensor gain $H(s)$ before subtraction.

### **Step 4 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 1.**

Replace $E(s)$ in the output equation:

$$
C(s) = G(s) \left[ R(s) - H(s) C(s) \right]
$$

**Explanation:** We now have a single equation in $C(s)$ and $R(s)$.

### **Step 5 — Distribute $G(s)$ over the bracket.**

$$
C(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) C(s)
$$

**Explanation:** Standard algebraic expansion.

### **Step 6 — Collect $C(s)$ terms on the left-hand side.**

$$
C(s) + G(s) H(s) C(s) = G(s) R(s)
$$

$$
C(s) \left[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \right] = G(s) R(s)
$$

**Explanation:** We factor out $C(s)$ to isolate it.

### **Step 7 — Solve for the closed-loop transfer function.**

$$
\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} \equiv T(s)
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Feedback on Overall Gain

### **Given:**

A forward gain $G(s)$ and a feedback gain $H(s)$. Original (open-loop) gain is $G(s)$; new (closed-loop) gain is $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$.

### **Step 1 — Express the closed-loop gain relative to the open-loop gain.**

Define the ratio:

$$
\frac{T(s)}{G(s)} = \frac{G(s) / \left[1 + G(s)H(s)\right]}{G(s)}
$$

### **Step 2 — Cancel $G(s)$ in numerator and denominator.**

$$
\frac{T(s)}{G(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}
$$

**Explanation:** This ratio is called the **desensitivity factor**.

### **Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Overall Gain is reduced by the factor } \dfrac{1}{1 + GH}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:**

- The overall gain **decreases** by the factor $1 + GH$.
- To restore the original gain, the designer must increase the forward-path gain $G$ by the same factor.
- This is the **fundamental trade-off** of feedback: gain reduction in exchange for sensitivity reduction, disturbance rejection, and bandwidth improvement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of Feedback on Disturbance Rejection

### **Given:**

A plant with transfer function $G_p(s)$, a controller $G_c(s)$, and a disturbance $D(s)$ injected at the plant input. Forward path is $G(s) = G_c(s) G_p(s)$ and feedback is $H(s)$.

### **Step 1 — Write the system equations including the disturbance.**

The output is the sum of two contributions:

$$
C(s) = G(s) E(s) + G_p(s) D(s)
$$

**Explanation:** Disturbance bypasses the controller and enters the plant directly.

### **Step 2 — Express the error signal.**

$$
E(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)
$$

### **Step 3 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 1.**

$$
C(s) = G(s) \left[ R(s) - H(s) C(s) \right] + G_p(s) D(s)
$$

### **Step 4 — Distribute and collect $C(s)$ terms.**

$$
C(s) + G(s) H(s) C(s) = G(s) R(s) + G_p(s) D(s)
$$

$$
C(s) \left[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \right] = G(s) R(s) + G_p(s) D(s)
$$

### **Step 5 — Solve for $C(s)$.**

$$
C(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} R(s) + \frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} D(s)
$$

### **Step 6 — Read off the disturbance transfer function.**

$$
\frac{C(s)}{D(s)} \bigg|_{R(s)=0} = \frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:**

- Compared to open-loop, where the disturbance transfer is just $G_p(s)$, the closed-loop attenuates the disturbance by the factor $1 + GH$.
- **Larger loop gain = better disturbance rejection.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effect of Feedback on Bandwidth

Consider a simple first-order plant:

$$
G(s) = \frac{K}{1 + \tau s}
$$

with unity feedback $H(s) = 1$.

### **Step 1 — Form the closed-loop transfer function.**

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{K / (1 + \tau s)}{1 + K / (1 + \tau s)}
$$

### **Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + \tau s)$.**

$$
T(s) = \frac{K}{(1 + \tau s) + K}
$$

### **Step 3 — Factor out $K$ from denominator.**

$$
T(s) = \frac{K}{(1 + K) + \tau s} = \frac{K / (1 + K)}{1 + \dfrac{\tau}{1 + K} s}
$$

### **Step 4 — Identify the new DC gain and time constant.**

$$
\boxed{T(s) = \frac{K_{cl}}{1 + \tau_{cl} s}}
$$

where:

$$
K_{cl} = \frac{K}{1 + K} \quad \text{(new DC gain)}
$$

$$
\tau_{cl} = \frac{\tau}{1 + K} \quad \text{(new time constant)}
$$

### **Step 5 — Note that the closed-loop bandwidth increases.**

The bandwidth of a first-order system equals the reciprocal of its time constant:

$$
\text{BW}_{cl} = \frac{1}{\tau_{cl}} = \frac{1 + K}{\tau}
$$

Compared to the open-loop bandwidth:

$$
\text{BW}_{ol} = \frac{1}{\tau}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Bandwidth increases by factor } (1 + K)}
$$

This is the **third major benefit of feedback**: simultaneously reducing sensitivity and increasing bandwidth at the cost of reduced gain.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Open-Loop System** | **Closed-Loop System** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Feedback path** | Absent | Present |
| **Accuracy** | Low, depends on calibration | High, self-correcting |
| **Disturbance rejection** | Poor — disturbance reaches output | Excellent — reduced by factor $1 + GH$ |
| **Sensitivity to parameter variation** | High | Reduced by factor $1 + GH$ |
| **Bandwidth** | Limited by plant | Increased by factor $1 + GH$ |
| **Stability** | Guaranteed stable | Requires careful design |
| **Cost and complexity** | Low | High (sensors + signal conditioning) |
| **Design effort** | Minimal | Rigorous (Bode, Nyquist, Root Locus) |
| **Transient response** | Not adjustable directly | Tunable via controller design |
| **Example** | Electric iron, traffic timer | Cruise control, autopilot |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Q:** Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Open-Loop System** | **Closed-Loop System** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Feedback** | Absent | Present |
| **Accuracy** | Low | High |
| **Stability** | Guaranteed | Requires analysis |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Example** | Electric iron | Automatic electric iron with thermostat |

**Examples in detail:**

- *Open-loop:* An **electric iron** heats for a fixed time determined by the user-set knob. There is no temperature sensor, so once the coil is calibrated, it cannot compensate if the room temperature changes or if the iron is left unattended.
- *Closed-loop:* A **refrigerator** uses a temperature sensor (feedback) to compare the actual cabinet temperature with the user-set reference. The compressor turns on or off based on the error, maintaining the desired temperature regardless of how often the door is opened.

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**Q:** What is a feedback control system? Why is negative feedback preferred over positive feedback?

**Model Answer:**

A **feedback control system** is one in which a portion of the output is measured and returned to the input to influence the subsequent behaviour of the system. The comparison between the reference input $R(s)$ and the feedback signal $B(s)$ produces an **error signal** $E(s) = R(s) \pm B(s)$, which then drives the controller.

**Why negative feedback is preferred:**

- **Stability:** Negative feedback produces the characteristic equation $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, which, when properly designed, yields a stable closed-loop system. Positive feedback produces $1 - G(s)H(s) = 0$, which is generally unstable for $G(s)H(s) > 1$.
- **Sensitivity reduction:** Negative feedback reduces sensitivity to plant parameter variations by the factor $1 + GH$.
- **Disturbance rejection:** Negative feedback attenuates disturbance effects by the factor $1 + GH$.
- **Linearization:** Negative feedback linearizes the effective behaviour of non-linear plants.
- **Bandwidth improvement:** Negative feedback increases the closed-loop bandwidth.

Positive feedback is only deliberately used in **oscillator circuits** (e.g., Wien bridge, RC phase-shift oscillators) where sustained oscillation is the desired output, **not** regulation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Module 1]`**

**Q:** (a) Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a negative-feedback control system having forward-path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback-path transfer function $H(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity-feedback system has a forward-path transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$. For a unit-step input, determine (i) the closed-loop transfer function, (ii) the steady-state error, and (iii) the effect of feedback on the overall gain. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The derivation proceeds in seven logical steps as derived in **Section 3.1** above.

**Step 1:** Output equation in terms of error signal:

$$
C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)
$$

**Step 2:** Error equation at summing junction:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - B(s)
$$

**Step 3:** Feedback signal expression:

$$
B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Step 3 into Step 2:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute Step 4 into Step 1:

$$
C(s) = G(s) \left[ R(s) - H(s) C(s) \right]
$$

**Step 6:** Distribute and collect $C(s)$ terms:

$$
C(s) \left[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \right] = G(s) R(s)
$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the closed-loop transfer function:

$$
\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}
$$

**Significance:** The denominator $1 + G(s)H(s)$ is called the **return difference** and determines closed-loop stability. Its roots (closed-loop poles) must lie in the left-half of the $s$-plane for stability.

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Given: Unity feedback so $H(s) = 1$, forward path $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$, input is unit-step $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$.

**(i) Closed-loop transfer function:**

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} = \frac{10 / \left[s(s+2)\right]}{1 + 10 / \left[s(s+2)\right]}
$$

**Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(s+2)$:**

$$
T(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2) + 10} = \frac{10}{s^{2} + 2s + 10}
$$

$$
\boxed{T(s) = \frac{10}{s^{2} + 2s + 10}}
$$

**(ii) Steady-state error for unit step input:**

The error transfer function for unity feedback is:

$$
\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)}
$$

Substituting $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$:

$$
\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + 10 / \left[s(s+2)\right]} = \frac{s(s+2)}{s(s+2) + 10} = \frac{s^{2} + 2s}{s^{2} + 2s + 10}
$$

By the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{s^{2} + 2s}{s^{2} + 2s + 10} \cdot \frac{1}{s}
$$

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s^{2} + 2s}{s^{2} + 2s + 10} = \frac{0}{10} = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{e_{ss} = 0}
$$

**(iii) Effect of feedback on overall gain:**

The closed-loop DC gain (at $s = 0$) is:

$$
T(0) = \frac{10}{10} = 1
$$

The open-loop DC gain would be infinite (because $G(0)$ has a pole at $s = 0$, i.e., an integrator). With unity feedback, the integrator is "tamed" to a finite gain of unity. **Feedback converts an infinite-gain open-loop system into a finite, well-controlled closed-loop system**, which is the most striking practical benefit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Module 1]`**

**Q:** (a) Explain the classification of control systems based on (i) structure, (ii) linearity, (iii) time behaviour, and (iv) signal type. Give one example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a block diagram, derive the transfer function of a closed-loop system and show that feedback (i) reduces sensitivity to plant variations, (ii) attenuates disturbances, and (iii) increases bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Structure-based classification:**

- **Open-loop:** Control action is independent of output. Example: **electric toaster**.
- **Closed-loop (feedback):** Control action depends on output via feedback. Example: **automatic washing machine** with water-level sensor.

**(ii) Linearity-based classification:**

- **Linear:** Satisfies superposition and homogeneity. Example: **RLC electrical circuit** with small-signal inputs.
- **Non-linear:** Does not obey superposition. Example: **saturating amplifier** or **diode clipper**.

**(iii) Time-behaviour classification:**

- **Time-invariant (LTI):** Parameters do not change with time. Example: **fixed-mass spring-damper system**.
- **Time-varying:** Parameters change with time. Example: **rocket with depleting fuel mass**.

**(iv) Signal-type classification:**

- **Continuous-time:** Signals defined for all $t$. Example: **analog electronic filter**.
- **Discrete-time:** Signals defined only at discrete instants. Example: **digital PID controller in a microcontroller**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Block diagram:** As shown in **Diagram 2 of Section 4.2**.

**Transfer function derivation:** As in **Derivation 1 of Section 3.1**, yielding:

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}
$$

**(i) Sensitivity reduction:**

Let $G$ change by $\Delta G$. The fractional change in $T$ is:

$$
\frac{\Delta T / T}{\Delta G / G} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}
$$

Since $1 + GH > 1$ for stable systems, the **relative sensitivity is reduced** by the feedback factor.

**(ii) Disturbance rejection:**

As derived in **Section 3.3**, the disturbance transfer function is:

$$
\frac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}
$$

Compared to open-loop where it is simply $G_p(s)$, the disturbance effect is **attenuated by the factor $1 + GH$**.

**(iii) Bandwidth increase:**

As derived in **Section 3.4** for a first-order plant with $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{1 + \tau s}$ and unity feedback:

$$
\tau_{cl} = \frac{\tau}{1 + K}, \quad \text{BW}_{cl} = \frac{1 + K}{\tau}
$$

The bandwidth is **increased by the factor $1 + K$** compared to open-loop.

**Conclusion:** Feedback trades a reduction in overall gain for simultaneous improvements in sensitivity, disturbance rejection, and bandwidth — making it indispensable in high-performance control engineering.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign at the summing junction (–1 mark):** The summing junction is *negative feedback*. Forgetting the minus sign gives $T(s) = G / (1 - GH)$, which is the *positive feedback* formula. Examiners immediately dock one full mark for this.
> 2. **Not writing the final boxed formula explicitly (–1 mark):** Even if the derivation is correct, examiners require the final result in a **box**. An unboxed answer is treated as incomplete.
> 3. **Confusing $G_c(s)$ with $G(s)$ (–1 mark):** $G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$ is the *total forward path*. Writing the closed-loop TF as $\dfrac{G_c(s)}{1 + G_c(s) H(s)}$ without including $G_p(s)$ is incorrect.
> 4. **Mixing up open-loop and closed-loop examples (–1 mark):** A *closed-loop* example must have a **sensor** and a **feedback path**. A washing machine with a fixed timer is open-loop; only a washing machine with a *water-level sensor* or *clothes-weight sensor* is closed-loop.
> 5. **Using Final Value Theorem without checking stability (–2 marks):** FVT applies only to stable systems. Always verify that all closed-loop poles lie in the open left-half $s$-plane before computing steady-state error.
> 6. **Not specifying the type of feedback (–1 mark):** Always write "**negative feedback assumed**" when using the formula $T(s) = G / (1 + GH)$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two fundamental types of control systems:** Open-loop (no feedback) and Closed-loop (with feedback).
- **Open-loop systems** are simple, cheap, and stable but inaccurate and unable to reject disturbances.
- **Closed-loop systems** are accurate, robust, and disturbance-rejecting but complex, costly, and risk instability.
- **Canonical closed-loop transfer function (must be memorized):**

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}
$$

- **Error signal formula:** $E(s) = R(s) - B(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$.
- **Loop gain:** $L(s) = G(s) H(s)$.
- **Sensitivity reduction factor:** $\dfrac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$.
- **Disturbance rejection factor:** $\dfrac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$.
- **Bandwidth increase factor:** $1 + GH$ for a first-order plant.
- **Steady-state error:** Compute using $\displaystyle e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$ **only after verifying stability**.
- **Negative feedback is preferred** for regulation, tracking, and disturbance rejection.
- **Positive feedback is reserved for oscillators** (e.g., Wien bridge oscillator).
- **Always draw the block diagram** before writing equations — it earns you at least 1–2 marks of method credit even if subsequent algebra has errors.
- **Always box the final formula** — examiners expect it.
- **Five key blocks in a closed-loop system:** Reference, Comparator, Controller, Plant, Sensor — recall them in this order for descriptive answers.
- **Classification hierarchy:** Open/Closed → Linear/Non-linear → LTI/Time-varying → Continuous/Discrete → SISO/MIMO.
- **Real-world closed-loop examples to memorize:** Cruise control, autopilot, ABS, refrigerator thermostat, voltage stabilizer, CNC machine tool.
- **Real-world open-loop examples to memorize:** Electric iron, bread toaster, traffic signal timer, ceiling fan regulator, automatic washing machine (with fixed timer).
- **Sign convention at summing junction:** $e = r - b$ (negative feedback is the default).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT631 - NETWORK AND LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Examples of Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Control System** is a deliberate interconnection of components forming a system configuration that will provide a desired system response. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1), a control system is an arrangement of physical elements organized to command, direct, or regulate itself or another system to achieve a specific objective.

In rigorous mathematical terms, a control system is a mapping:

$$ y(t) = f\big(r(t), \text{plant dynamics}, \text{controller}, \text{feedback path}\big) $$

where $r(t)$ is the reference input, $y(t)$ is the controlled output, and the plant dynamics are governed by a set of linear or nonlinear differential equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PEERT631 mandates that students must be able to *identify, classify, and model* classical control system examples drawn from electrical, mechanical, thermal, hydraulic, and electromechanical domains. The examples form the foundation for the transfer function and block diagram algebra covered in Module 2.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Car Driver (Closed-Loop Example):**
Imagine you are driving a car and wish to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** despite varying road slopes, wind resistance, and traffic. The components map as:
- **Desired speed (60 km/h)** → Reference input $r(t)$
- **Speedometer reading** → Sensor / Feedback element
- **Your eyes + brain** → Comparator + Controller
- **Your foot on the accelerator** → Actuator
- **The car** → Plant
- **Actual speed** → Output $y(t)$

If the road inclines, the car slows; the driver observes the drop on the speedometer and presses the accelerator harder. This *self-correcting* behaviour is the very essence of feedback control.

**Analogy 2 — The Hand-Washing Tap (Open-Loop Example):**
Consider a hand-operated tap. You turn the knob a fixed amount expecting water at a desired pressure. The tap has no sensor to verify if the water pressure matches your expectation — the output is assumed to be correct. This is **open-loop control** — simple, cheap, but inaccurate under disturbances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exams:** If a system has feedback (sensor → comparator → correction), it is *closed-loop*. If there is no feedback path, it is *open-loop*. A **servo system** is a subset of closed-loop systems where the output is a mechanical position, velocity, or acceleration.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- **SI Unit of Electrical Current:** Ampere (A) — denoted by $i(t)$.
- **SI Unit of Force:** Newton (N) — denoted by $F(t)$.
- **SI Unit of Temperature:** Kelvin (K), but commonly used in °C for thermal control examples.
- **Reference Temperature for Standard Conditions:** $T_0 = \mathbf{25^\circ C}$.
- **Standard Gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$ — used in liquid-level control examples.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in PEERT631

The KTU 2024 Scheme frames this as the *gateway topic*. Every transfer function you derive in Module 2, every stability criterion you apply in Module 3, and every state-space model in Module 5 begins with a **physical system**. Without understanding the examples (the RC circuit, the mass-spring-damper, the armature-controlled DC motor), the subsequent mathematical machinery has no physical anchor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Control Systems

Control systems are classified along several axes, all of which are high-yield for KTU exams:

**A. By Feedback Structure**
- **Open-Loop Control System:** No feedback. Output is not measured. Disturbances are not rejected.
- **Closed-Loop (Feedback) Control System:** Output is measured and fed back to a comparator. Self-correcting against disturbances.

**B. By Linearity**
- **Linear Control System:** Satisfies the principle of superposition (homogeneity + additivity). Governed by linear ODEs.
- **Non-Linear Control System:** Does not obey superposition. Includes saturation, dead-zone, hysteresis.

**C. By Time-Behaviour**
- **Continuous-Time:** Signals are defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$.
- **Discrete-Time:** Signals defined only at sampling instants $t = kT$, $k \in \mathbb{Z}$.

**D. By Number of Inputs/Outputs (MIMO vs SISO)**
- **SISO (Single-Input Single-Output):** One input, one output (most Module 1 examples).
- **MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output):** Multiple inputs/outputs (introduced briefly, expanded in Module 5).

### 2.2 KTU-Style Real-World Examples (High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly demands coverage of the following examples. Each maps a physical process to a control system.

#### Example 1: Room Temperature Controller (Thermal, Closed-Loop)
- **Plant:** Room air mass.
- **Sensor:** Thermostat (bimetallic strip or thermistor).
- **Actuator:** Heater coil (heats) or air conditioner (cools).
- **Disturbance:** Outside temperature variation, door opening.

#### Example 2: Electric Iron (Thermal, Open-Loop, with Adjustment)
- **Plant:** Sole plate of the iron.
- **Control Input:** Manual setting of heating element power (e.g., "cotton", "linen").
- **No Sensor:** The iron does not measure the actual sole-plate temperature.

#### Example 3: Armature-Controlled DC Motor (Electromechanical, Closed-Loop)
- **Plant:** DC motor (rotor + load).
- **Input:** Armature voltage $v_a(t)$.
- **Output:** Angular position $\theta(t)$ or speed $\omega(t)$.
- **Feedback:** Tachogenerator or encoder measures $\omega(t)$.

#### Example 4: Liquid-Level Control System (Hydraulic, Closed-Loop)
- **Plant:** Tank with inflow $Q_{in}(t)$ and outflow $Q_{out}(t)$.
- **Sensor:** Float-based level sensor.
- **Actuator:** Inlet valve.

#### Example 5: Satellite Tracking Antenna (Electro-Mechanical, Servo, Closed-Loop)
- **Plant:** Antenna dish + drive motors.
- **Input:** Desired satellite bearing.
- **Output:** Actual antenna angle.
- **Feedback:** Shaft encoder on antenna axis.

#### Example 6: Traffic Signal Control (Discrete/Logical, Often Open-Loop in basic form)
- Pre-programmed timing logic; can be upgraded to closed-loop with inductive loop vehicle sensors.

#### Example 7: Robotic Arm Joint (Electromechanical, MIMO, Closed-Loop)
- Multiple joints, each an independent closed-loop position servo, but with coupling.

#### Example 8: Voltage Stabilizer (Electrical, Closed-Loop)
- **Plant:** Output voltage across load.
- **Sensor:** Sampling resistor divider.
- **Actuator:** Servo-tap changer on a transformer.

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Example

| Example | Physical Domain | Why It Is Classified as a Control System |
|---|---|---|
| Room Heater | Thermal | Has a setpoint, a sensor, and a corrective action — textbook feedback loop. |
| DC Motor | Electromechanical | Input voltage → output rotation; position/speed is measured and regulated. |
| Liquid Level | Hydraulic | Level setpoint vs. measured level dictates valve opening. |
| Traffic Signal | Logical/Temporal | Time-based or sensor-based command issued to lamps. |
| Voltage Stabilizer | Electrical | Output voltage regulated despite input voltage variation. |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (For Examples Covered in Module 1)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every standard formula that the KTU 2024 PEERT631 Module 1 exam can expect a student to recognize. The transfer function form $G(s)$ is given in each case.

| # | Physical System | Governing Equation | Transfer Function $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$ | Standard Notation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RC Series Circuit (Electrical) | $RC \dfrac{dv_c}{dt} + v_c = v_{in}$ | $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{RCs + 1}$ | $R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in F |
| 2 | RL Series Circuit (Electrical) | $L \dfrac{di}{dt} + Ri = v_{in}$ | $G(s) = \dfrac{1 \vert s \cdot 0 + 1/L}{s + R/L}$ | $L$ in H, $R$ in $\Omega$ |
| 3 | RLC Series Circuit (Electrical) | $LC\dfrac{d^2 v_c}{dt^2} + RC\dfrac{dv_c}{dt} + v_c = v_{in}$ | $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{LCs^2 + RCs + 1}$ | — |
| 4 | Mass-Spring-Damper (Mechanical, Translation) | $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F(t)$ | $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{Ms^2 + Bs + K}$ | $M$ in kg, $B$ in Ns/m, $K$ in N/m |
| 5 | Torsional Spring-Damper (Rotational Mechanical) | $J\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} + K\theta = T(t)$ | $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{Js^2 + Bs + K}$ | $J$ in kg·m² |
| 6 | Armature-Controlled DC Motor (Speed Output) | Differential equation of motor | $G(s) = \dfrac{K_m}{(R_a + L_a s)(Js + B) + K_b K_m}$ | $K_m, K_b$ motor constants |
| 7 | Armature-Controlled DC Motor (Position Output) | As above with extra integrator | $G(s) = \dfrac{K_m}{s \big[(R_a + L_a s)(Js + B) + K_b K_m\big]}$ | — |
| 8 | Thermal System (First-Order Heating) | $C \dfrac{dT}{dt} + \dfrac{T}{R_{th}} = Q(t)$ | $G(s) = \dfrac{R_{th}}{R_{th}Cs + 1}$ | $C$ = thermal capacitance, $R_{th}$ = thermal resistance |
| 9 | Liquid Level (Linear Tank) | $A \dfrac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ | $G(s) = \dfrac{R}{\tau s + 1}$ where $\tau = AR$ | $A$ = tank area, $R$ = flow resistance |
| 10 | Potentiometer (Transducer) | $v_o = \dfrac{v_i}{x_{max}} x$ | $G(s) = K_p$ (pure gain) | $K_p = v_i / x_{max}$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **RC and RLC circuits** are the workhorses of filter design, signal conditioning, and analog computers.
- **Mass-spring-damper models** describe vehicle suspensions, building seismic dampers, and robotic manipulators.
- **DC motor models** are central to CNC machines, hard-disk drives, and electric vehicles.
- **Thermal system models** govern HVAC design, chemical reactor control, and chip thermal management in CPUs.
- **Liquid-level models** are fundamental to process control in chemical, water-treatment, and petrochemical plants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

The KTU 2024 PEERT631 Module 1 routinely asks students to derive the transfer function of a *basic* example system. The two most-tested derivations are presented in full, step-by-step.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Transfer Function of a Series RC Circuit (First-Order Electrical System)

**Physical Setup:** A resistor $R$ and capacitor $C$ are connected in series across a voltage source $v_{in}(t)$. The output is the voltage across the capacitor $v_c(t)$.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):**

$$ v_{in}(t) = v_R(t) + v_C(t) $$

**Step 2 — Substitute element equations:**

Using the resistor relation $v_R(t) = R \cdot i(t)$ and the capacitor relation $i(t) = C \dfrac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$, we substitute:

$$ v_{in}(t) = R \cdot i(t) + v_C(t) = RC \cdot \dfrac{dv_C(t)}{dt} + v_C(t) $$

**Step 3 — Write the governing differential equation explicitly:**

$$ RC \cdot \dfrac{dv_C(t)}{dt} + v_C(t) = v_{in}(t) $$

**Step 4 — Take the Laplace Transform (assuming zero initial conditions):**

Using $\mathcal{L}\left\{ \dfrac{dv_C}{dt} \right\} = s V_C(s)$ and $\mathcal{L}\{v_C(t)\} = V_C(s)$:

$$ RC \cdot s \cdot V_C(s) + V_C(s) = V_{in}(s) $$

**Step 5 — Factor out $V_C(s)$:**

$$ V_C(s) \cdot (RCs + 1) = V_{in}(s) $$

**Step 6 — Solve for the transfer function $G(s) = V_C(s) / V_{in}(s)$:**

$$ G(s) = \dfrac{V_C(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{1}{RCs + 1} $$

**Step 7 — Identify the time constant $\tau$:**

By definition, $\tau = RC$. Hence the canonical form is:

$$ G(s) = \dfrac{1}{\tau s + 1}, \quad \text{where } \tau = RC \text{ seconds} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently forget the **zero initial condition** assumption. The Laplace transform of $\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$ is $sV_C(s) - v_C(0^-)$. If the initial capacitor voltage is non-zero, the term $v_C(0^-)$ survives in the algebra and the transfer function loses its meaning. KTU examiners are explicit: *for transfer functions, initial conditions are zero by definition.*

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Transfer Function of a Mass-Spring-Damper System (Second-Order Mechanical System)

**Physical Setup:** A mass $M$ (kg) is connected to a fixed wall via a spring of stiffness $K$ (N/m) and a viscous damper of damping coefficient $B$ (Ns/m). An external force $F(t)$ is applied. The displacement of the mass is $x(t)$.

**Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram Forces:**
Three forces act on the mass:
- **Inertial force:** $M \ddot{x}(t)$ (opposing motion, by Newton's second law).
- **Damping force:** $B \dot{x}(t)$ (opposing velocity, by viscous friction).
- **Spring restoring force:** $K x(t)$ (opposing displacement).

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law (D'Alembert's principle):**

$$ F(t) = M \ddot{x}(t) + B \dot{x}(t) + K x(t) $$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into standard form:**

$$ M \ddot{x}(t) + B \dot{x}(t) + K x(t) = F(t) $$

**Step 4 — Take the Laplace transform under zero initial conditions:**

$$ M s^2 X(s) + B s X(s) + K X(s) = F(s) $$

**Step 5 — Factor out $X(s)$:**

$$ X(s) \cdot (Ms^2 + Bs + K) = F(s) $$

**Step 6 — Solve for the transfer function $G(s) = X(s) / F(s)$:**

$$ G(s) = \dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Ms^2 + Bs + K} $$

**Step 7 — Convert to standard second-order form by dividing by $K$:**

$$ G(s) = \dfrac{1/K}{\dfrac{M}{K} s^2 + \dfrac{B}{K} s + 1} = \dfrac{1/K}{\dfrac{s^2}{\omega_n^2} + \dfrac{2\zeta s}{\omega_n} + 1} $$

where the undamped natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$ are:

$$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}} \; \text{rad/s}, \qquad \zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The mass-spring-damper is mathematically identical in form to the **series RLC circuit** (Section 2.4, Row 3). The mappings are: $M \leftrightarrow L$, $B \leftrightarrow R$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/C$. This duality is the foundation of *electromechanical analogies* — force-voltage analogy and force-current analogy. KTU Module 2 builds on this directly.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Transfer Function of a Simple Liquid-Level System (First-Order Hydraulic System)

**Physical Setup:** A cylindrical tank of cross-sectional area $A$ (m²) receives inflow $Q_{in}(t)$ (m³/s) at the top and discharges through a valve with flow resistance $R$ (s/m²) at the bottom. The outflow $Q_{out}(t)$ depends linearly on the liquid head $h(t)$ (m): $Q_{out} = h/R$.

**Step 1 — Mass balance (Volume conservation for an incompressible liquid):**

$$ A \dfrac{dh(t)}{dt} = Q_{in}(t) - Q_{out}(t) $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the linear outflow expression:**

$$ A \dfrac{dh(t)}{dt} = Q_{in}(t) - \dfrac{h(t)}{R} $$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$ AR \cdot \dfrac{dh(t)}{dt} + h(t) = R \cdot Q_{in}(t) $$

**Step 4 — Take Laplace transform (zero initial head):**

$$ AR s H(s) + H(s) = R Q_{in}(s) $$

**Step 5 — Factor $H(s)$:**

$$ H(s) \cdot (ARs + 1) = R Q_{in}(s) $$

**Step 6 — Solve for the transfer function:**

$$ G(s) = \dfrac{H(s)}{Q_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{R}{ARs + 1} $$

**Step 7 — Identify the time constant $\tau = AR$ and the steady-state gain $K = R$:**

$$ G(s) = \dfrac{R}{\tau s + 1}, \quad \tau = AR, \; K = R $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Many students incorrectly treat $A$ as gain instead of $R$. The steady-state (DC) gain of a level system is $R$, the *flow resistance*, not the cross-sectional area. The cross-sectional area $A$ enters only the time constant.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| System | Order | Poles (roots of denominator) | Natural Frequency $\omega_n$ | Damping Ratio $\zeta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RC Circuit | 1 | $s = -1/RC$ | Not applicable (first-order) | Not applicable |
| RLC Circuit | 2 | $s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$, $\alpha = R/(2L)$ | $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ | $\zeta = (R/2)\sqrt{C/L}$ |
| Mass-Spring-Damper | 2 | $s_{1,2} = -\zeta \omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$ | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$ | $\zeta = B / (2\sqrt{MK})$ |
| Liquid Level | 1 | $s = -1/(AR)$ | Not applicable | Not applicable |
| Thermal First-Order | 1 | $s = -1/(R_{th} C_{th})$ | Not applicable | Not applicable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical feedback control structure, which is the unifying framework behind *every* example in PEERT631 Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reference Input R(s)"] --> B["Summing Junction e(t) = r - b"]
    B --> C["Controller Gc(s)"]
    C --> D["Actuator Ga(s)"]
    D --> E["Plant Gp(s)"]
    E --> F["Output Y(s)"]
    F --> G["Feedback Sensor H(s)"]
    G --> B
    H["Disturbance D(s)"] -.-> E
    I["Noise N(s)"] -.-> G
    classDef block fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,font-size:14px
    classDef signal fill:#f4b400,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000000,font-size:13px
    classDef disturb fill:#c0392b,stroke:#641e16,color:#ffffff,font-size:12px
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G block
    class H,I disturb
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **forward path** is: $R(s) \to e(s) \to U(s) \to V(s) \to Y(s)$.
- The **feedback path** is: $Y(s) \to B(s) \to$ (back to the summer).
- Disturbances $D(s)$ enter the plant directly. Sensor noise $N(s)$ enters the feedback path.
- The **error signal** $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$ drives the controller.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram of an Armature-Controlled DC Motor (Position Servo)

This is the most-tested electromechanical example in KTU 2024 PEERT631.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Va(s) Armature Voltage"] --> B["Armature Circuit La Ra s Ra"]
    B --> C["Torque Constant Km produces T(s)"]
    C --> D["Mechanical Load Js plus B"]
    D --> E["Angular Velocity Omega s"]
    E --> F["Integrator 1 over s"]
    F --> G["Angular Position Theta s"]
    G --> H["Tachogenerator Kb returns to summer"]
    H --> A
    classDef motor fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,font-size:13px
    classDef meas fill:#27ae60,stroke:#145a32,color:#ffffff,font-size:13px
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G motor
    class H meas
```

**Key transfers:**
- Armature electrical sub-system: $G_e(s) = \dfrac{1}{L_a s + R_a}$
- Motor electromechanical constant: $K_m$ (Nm/A)
- Mechanical sub-system: $G_m(s) = \dfrac{1}{Js + B}$
- Back-EMF constant: $K_b$ (V·s/rad)
- Output $\omega(s)$ relationship: $G_\omega(s) = \dfrac{K_m}{(L_a s + R_a)(Js + B) + K_m K_b}$

---

### 4.3 Functional Flow of Examples Classification (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Control System Examples Module 1"] --> B["Open Loop Systems"]
    A --> C["Closed Loop Systems"]
    B --> B1["Electric Iron"]
    B --> B2["Traffic Signal Timer"]
    B --> B3["Bread Toaster"]
    C --> C1["Room Temperature Controller"]
    C --> C2["DC Motor Position Servo"]
    C --> C3["Liquid Level Tank"]
    C --> C4["Voltage Stabilizer"]
    C --> C5["Satellite Antenna Tracker"]
    C1 --> D1["Thermal Domain"]
    C2 --> D2["Electromechanical Domain"]
    C3 --> D3["Hydraulic Domain"]
    C4 --> D4["Electrical Domain"]
    C5 --> D5["Servo Domain"]
    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,font-size:14px
    classDef branch fill:#f4b400,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000000,font-size:13px
    classDef example fill:#27ae60,stroke:#145a32,color:#ffffff,font-size:12px
    classDef domain fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#43225c,color:#ffffff,font-size:12px
    class A root
    class B,C,B1,B2,B3,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 branch
    class D1,D2,D3,D4,D5 domain
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic: Conceptual Sensor-Plant-Actuator Flow for the Room Heater Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["User Thermostat Setpoint Tref"] --> B["Comparator e = Tref minus Tfb"]
    B --> C["Controller ON OFF or PID"]
    C --> D["Heater Actuator Power"]
    D --> E["Room Plant Thermal RC"]
    E --> F["Room Temperature Tactual"]
    F --> G["Thermistor Sensor"]
    G --> B
    H["Outside Temperature Disturbance"] -.-> E
    classDef block fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,font-size:13px
    classDef disturb fill:#c0392b,stroke:#641e16,color:#ffffff,font-size:12px
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G block
    class H disturb
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 1 Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating "input" with "actuator signal":** Many students answer "input is the heater coil" — *wrong*. The input is the setpoint reference. The actuator is the device that *receives* the controller output.
> 2. **Skipping units in numerical problems:** PEERT631 examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for missing SI units. Always write $R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in F, $M$ in kg, $K$ in N/m.
> 3. **Forgetting zero initial conditions when defining transfer functions:** A transfer function is undefined with non-zero initial conditions. Always state the assumption.
> 4. **Mixing up the steady-state gain:** In the liquid level system, gain is $R$, not $A$. In the thermal system, gain is $R_{th}$, not $C_{th}$. Memorize the correct *role* of each parameter.

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a control system. Distinguish clearly between an open-loop and a closed-loop control system with one suitable example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A control system is an arrangement of physical components interconnected in such a way as to command, regulate, or direct itself or another system to achieve a prescribed objective (desired output).

**Open-Loop Control System:** A system in which the control action is independent of the output. There is no feedback path.

*Example:* An electric iron. The user sets a heat level; the system does not sense the actual sole-plate temperature.

**Closed-Loop Control System:** A system in which the control action depends on the output through a feedback path. The output is continuously compared with a reference, and the error drives the controller.

*Example:* A room temperature controller with a thermostat. The actual room temperature is sensed and compared to the setpoint; the heater is switched on/off based on the error.

**Distinguishing Table:**

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Poor | High |
| Sensitivity to disturbance | High | Low |
| Stability | Generally stable | Can become unstable |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Example | Electric iron | Room thermostat |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for distinction, 1 for examples.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List any six real-world examples of control systems and identify the physical domain (electrical / mechanical / thermal / hydraulic / electromechanical) for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Example | Physical Domain |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Room temperature controller (thermostat) | Thermal |
| 2 | Armature-controlled DC motor | Electromechanical |
| 3 | Liquid-level control in a tank | Hydraulic |
| 4 | RC low-pass filter used in audio equipment | Electrical |
| 5 | Vehicle suspension (mass-spring-damper) | Mechanical |
| 6 | Voltage stabilizer / Automatic Voltage Regulator | Electrical |
| 7 (Bonus) | Satellite dish tracking antenna | Electromechanical (servo) |

*(3 marks: 0.5 for each correct pairing, examples must be distinct.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module contains one 14-mark question. You are given an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** — answer *either*, not both. Each question has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Derive the transfer function of an armature-controlled DC motor. State clearly all the assumptions and define every symbol used. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
- Armature current $i_a(t)$ produces a torque proportional to $i_a$ (linear magnetic field, no saturation).
- Back-EMF $e_b(t)$ is proportional to angular velocity $\omega(t)$.
- The motor drives a purely inertial + viscous load (rotor inertia $J$ and friction $B$).
- All initial conditions are zero (transfer function definition).
- Field current is held constant (separately excited).

**Symbol Definitions:**
- $v_a(t)$: Armature voltage (input), in V.
- $i_a(t)$: Armature current, in A.
- $R_a, L_a$: Armature resistance ($\Omega$) and inductance (H).
- $e_b(t)$: Back-EMF (V), with $e_b = K_b \omega(t)$, $K_b$ in V·s/rad.
- $T(t)$: Developed torque (Nm), with $T = K_m i_a(t)$, $K_m$ in Nm/A.
- $J$: Rotor + load inertia (kg·m²).
- $B$: Viscous friction coefficient (N·m·s/rad).
- $\omega(t) = d\theta/dt$: Angular velocity (rad/s).
- $\theta(t)$: Angular position (rad).

**Step 1 — Armature circuit KVL:**

$$ v_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \dfrac{di_a}{dt} + e_b(t) $$

Substituting $e_b = K_b \omega$:

$$ v_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \dfrac{di_a}{dt} + K_b \omega(t) $$

**Step 2 — Mechanical equation (Newton's second law for rotation):**

$$ T(t) = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega(t) $$

Substituting $T = K_m i_a$:

$$ K_m i_a(t) = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega(t) $$

**Step 3 — Laplace transform (zero ICs):**

$$ V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_b \Omega(s) $$

$$ K_m I_a(s) = (Js + B) \Omega(s) $$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $I_a(s)$:**

From the second equation: $I_a(s) = \dfrac{(Js + B) \Omega(s)}{K_m}$.

Substitute into the first:

$$ V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \dfrac{(Js + B) \Omega(s)}{K_m} + K_b \Omega(s) $$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\Omega(s) / V_a(s)$:**

$$ V_a(s) = \Omega(s) \left[ \dfrac{(R_a + L_a s)(Js + B)}{K_m} + K_b \right] $$

$$ \dfrac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{(R_a + L_a s)(Js + B) + K_m K_b} $$

**Final Transfer Function (Speed):**

$$ G_\omega(s) = \dfrac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{(L_a s + R_a)(Js + B) + K_m K_b} $$

**For Position Output** (add an integrator $1/s$):

$$ G_\theta(s) = \dfrac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{s \big[(L_a s + R_a)(Js + B) + K_m K_b\big]} $$

*(7 marks: 1 for assumptions and symbols, 4 for derivation steps, 1 for simplification, 1 for final transfer function.)*

---

**(b) For the mass-spring-damper system, derive the transfer function $X(s)/F(s)$ and express it in standard second-order form. Identify the natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Equation of motion:**

For a mass $M$ connected to a spring of stiffness $K$ and a viscous damper of coefficient $B$, with applied force $F(t)$ and displacement $x(t)$:

$$ M \ddot{x}(t) + B \dot{x}(t) + K x(t) = F(t) $$

**Step 2 — Take the Laplace transform (zero ICs):**

$$ M s^2 X(s) + B s X(s) + K X(s) = F(s) $$

**Step 3 — Factor $X(s)$:**

$$ X(s) (M s^2 + B s + K) = F(s) $$

**Step 4 — Solve for the transfer function:**

$$ G(s) = \dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{M s^2 + B s + K} $$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by $K$:**

$$ G(s) = \dfrac{1/K}{\dfrac{M}{K} s^2 + \dfrac{B}{K} s + 1} $$

**Step 6 — Compare with the standard second-order form:**

$$ G(s) = \dfrac{K_{ss}}{\dfrac{s^2}{\omega_n^2} + \dfrac{2\zeta s}{\omega_n} + 1} $$

Matching coefficients:

$$ \omega_n^2 = \dfrac{K}{M} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}} \; \text{rad/s} $$

$$ \dfrac{2\zeta}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{B}{K} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \zeta = \dfrac{B}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{MK}} = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}} $$

**Step 7 — Final canonical form:**

$$ G(s) = \dfrac{1/K}{(s/\omega_n)^2 + 2\zeta (s/\omega_n) + 1} $$

**Discussion of $\zeta$ regimes** (for conceptual completeness):
- $\zeta = 0$: Undamped oscillation at $\omega_n$.
- $0 < \zeta < 1$: Underdamped (decaying oscillations).
- $\zeta = 1$: Critically damped (fastest non-oscillatory response).
- $\zeta > 1$: Overdamped (slow, no overshoot).

*(7 marks: 1 for equation of motion, 2 for Laplace transform and transfer function, 2 for standard form conversion, 2 for $\omega_n, \zeta$ identification.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) Derive the transfer function $V_o(s)/V_i(s)$ of a series RLC circuit with output taken across the capacitor. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit description:** A resistor $R$, inductor $L$, and capacitor $C$ are connected in series. The input is $v_i(t)$ applied across the series combination. The output is the capacitor voltage $v_C(t) = v_o(t)$.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the loop:**

$$ v_i(t) = v_R(t) + v_L(t) + v_C(t) $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the element voltage-current relations:**

- $v_R(t) = R \cdot i(t)$
- $v_L(t) = L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$
- $i(t) = C \cdot \dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$

**Step 4 — Express everything in terms of $v_C(t)$:**

$$ v_i(t) = R \cdot C \dfrac{dv_C}{dt} + L \cdot C \dfrac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2} + v_C(t) $$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into the standard ODE:**

$$ LC \dfrac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2} + RC \dfrac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C = v_i(t) $$

**Step 6 — Take the Laplace transform (zero ICs):**

$$ LC s^2 V_C(s) + RC s V_C(s) + V_C(s) = V_i(s) $$

**Step 7 — Factor $V_C(s)$:**

$$ V_C(s) (LC s^2 + RC s + 1) = V_i(s) $$

**Step 8 — Solve for the transfer function:**

$$ \boxed{G(s) = \dfrac{V_C(s)}{V_i(s)} = \dfrac{1}{LC s^2 + RC s + 1}} $$

**Step 9 — Standard second-order form:**

$$ G(s) = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{s^2}{\omega_n^2} + \dfrac{2\zeta s}{\omega_n} + 1} $$

where:
$$ \omega_n = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \; \text{rad/s}, \qquad \zeta = \dfrac{R}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}} $$

*(7 marks: 1 for KVL, 2 for substitution, 1 for ODE, 1 for Laplace transform, 1 for transfer function, 1 for standard form.)*

---

**(b) Derive the transfer function of a simple liquid-level system. State all assumptions and identify the time constant and steady-state gain. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — System description:** A cylindrical tank of constant cross-sectional area $A$ (m²) receives an inflow $Q_{in}(t)$ (m³/s) at the top. The liquid discharges through a valve at the bottom. The output is the liquid level $h(t)$ (m).

**Assumptions:**
- The liquid is incompressible.
- The tank cross-section $A$ is constant with height.
