**(b) Loop:** A loop (also called a feedback loop) is a closed path that begins and ends at the same node, where no other node along the path is visited more than once. The product of all branch transmittances traversed along the loop is called the loop gain.

**(c) Non-Touching Loops:** Two or more loops in an SFG are called non-touching if they do **not share any common node** between them. The product of their individual loop gains contributes to the higher-order terms in the determinant $\Delta$ of the SFG.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State Mason's Gain Formula. Explain the meaning of each term with suitable notation.**

**Model Answer:**

Mason's Gain Formula expresses the overall transfer function of a linear system represented as a Signal Flow Graph as:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$$

Where:
- $C(s)$ = Output node variable (sink)
- $R(s)$ = Input node variable (source)
- $N$ = Total number of forward paths from input to output
- $P_k$ = Path factor (product of branch transmittances) of the $k^{th}$ forward path
- $\Delta$ = Determinant of the SFG, given by $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \cdots$ where $L_i$ are individual loop gains, $L_i L_j$ are products of two non-touching loops, and $L_i L_j L_k$ are products of three non-touching loops.
- $\Delta_k$ = Cofactor of the $k^{th}$ forward path = determinant of the residual SFG obtained after removing all nodes touched by the $k^{th}$ forward path.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For the system described by the following equations, construct the Signal Flow Graph and find the transfer function $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ using Mason's Gain Formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= R(s) \\
X_2 &= X_1 - H(s) X_5 \\
X_3 &= G_1(s) X_2 \\
X_4 &= X_3 + G_2(s) X_2 \\
X_5 &= X_4 - H(s) X_5 \\
C(s) &= X_5
\end{aligned}$$

**(b)** Explain why Mason's Gain Formula reduces to the standard closed-loop transfer function $\dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ for a single-loop unity feedback system.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Draw the SFG:**

The equations translate into the following branches:
- $R \to X_2$ with gain $1$
- $X_5 \to X_2$ with gain $-H(s)$ (negative feedback)
- $X_2 \to X_3$ with gain $G_1(s)$
- $X_2 \to X_4$ with gain $G_2(s)$
- $X_3 \to X_4$ with gain $1$
- $X_4 \to X_5$ with gain $1$
- $X_5 \to X_5$ self-loop with gain $-H(s)$

**Step 2 — Forward Paths from $R$ to $C(s) = X_5$:**

**Path 1:** $R \to X_2 \to X_3 \to X_4 \to X_5$
$$P_1 = (1)(G_1)(1)(1) = G_1(s)$$

**Path 2:** $R \to X_2 \to X_4 \to X_5$
$$P_2 = (1)(G_2)(1) = G_2(s)$$

So $N = 2$.

**Step 3 — Loops:**

**Loop 1:** $X_2 \to X_3 \to X_4 \to X_5 \to X_2$
$$L_1 = (G_1)(1)(1)(-H) = -G_1(s) H(s)$$

**Loop 2 (self-loop):** $X_5 \to X_5$
$$L_2 = -H(s)$$

So $M = 2$.

**Step 4 — Non-Touching Loops:** $L_1$ touches $X_5$. $L_2$ is at $X_5$. They share node $X_5$, so they **touch each other**. No non-touching pairs.

**Step 5 — Compute $\Delta$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 1 - (L_1 + L_2) \\
&= 1 - (-G_1 H - H) \\
&= 1 + G_1 H + H \\
&= 1 + H(s)(1 + G_1(s))
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\Delta_k$:**

**For $P_1$:** Path 1 touches $R, X_2, X_3, X_4, X_5$. Removing these nodes leaves nothing, so $\Delta_1 = 1$.

**For $P_2$:** Path 2 touches $R, X_2, X_4, X_5$. Removing these nodes leaves nothing, so $\Delta_2 = 1$.

**Step 7 — Apply Mason's Formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} &= \frac{P_1 \Delta_1 + P_2 \Delta_2}{\Delta} \\
&= \frac{G_1(s) + G_2(s)}{1 + H(s)(1 + G_1(s))}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_1(s) + G_2(s)}{1 + H(s) + G_1(s) H(s)}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks:**

For a single-loop unity negative feedback system, the SFG contains:
- One forward path: $R \to C$ with $P_1 = G(s)$
- One loop: $C \to R$ via feedback branch with $L_1 = -G(s)H(s)$

**Step 1:** Compute $\Delta$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 1 - L_1 \\
&= 1 - (-GH) \\
&= 1 + GH
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\Delta_1$: The forward path $P_1$ passes through both nodes of the loop. Removing these nodes eliminates the loop entirely, so $\Delta_1 = 1$.

**Step 3:** Apply Mason's Formula:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} &= \frac{P_1 \Delta_1}{\Delta} \\
&= \frac{G(s) \cdot 1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}
\end{aligned}$$

This is identical to the classical block diagram reduction result, confirming the **equivalence** between Mason's Gain Formula approach and block diagram reduction method.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Determine the transfer function $\dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$ for the SFG described below using Mason's Gain Formula:

- Forward paths: $P_1 = a$, $P_2 = bc$
- Loops: $L_1 = -ad$, $L_2 = -ce$, $L_3 = -bcf$
- Loop pairs: $L_1$ and $L_2$ do **not touch**.
- Loop triplet: $L_1, L_2, L_3$ do **not all simultaneously touch** (only $L_1$ and $L_2$ qualify as non-touching).

**(b)** Convert the block diagram below into an equivalent Signal Flow Graph and verify the transfer function using Mason's Gain Formula:

$$\text{Block Diagram: } R(s) \to [G_1] \to \oplus \to [G_2] \to C(s)$$
with feedback loop: $C(s) \to [H_1] \to \text{sum}_1$ and a secondary path $R(s) \to [G_3] \to \text{sum}_1$ feeding forward.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify Inputs:**
- Forward paths: $P_1 = a$, $P_2 = bc$
- Loops: $L_1 = -ad$, $L_2 = -ce$, $L_3 = -bcf$

**Step 2 — Identify Non-Touching Loop Combinations:**

- $L_1$ and $L_2$: Non-touching $\Rightarrow L_1 L_2 = (-ad)(-ce) = acde$
- $L_1$ and $L_3$: Touching (both pass through node $b$) $\Rightarrow$ not counted
- $L_2$ and $L_3$: Touching (both pass through node $c$) $\Rightarrow$ not counted
- $L_1, L_2, L_3$: Cannot form triplet since $L_3$ touches both $\Rightarrow$ not counted

**Step 3 — Compute $\Delta$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 1 - (L_1 + L_2 + L_3) + L_1 L_2 \\
&= 1 - (-ad - ce - bcf) + (acde) \\
&= 1 + ad + ce + bcf + acde
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta_k$:**

**For $P_1 = a$:** Path 1 passes through the nodes of $L_1$ only. Removing these nodes eliminates $L_1$ but leaves $L_2$ and $L_3$ intact.
$$\Delta_1 = 1 - L_2 - L_3 = 1 - (-ce) - (-bcf) = 1 + ce + bcf$$

**For $P_2 = bc$:** Path 2 passes through nodes of $L_2$ and $L_3$. Removing these eliminates both loops. $L_1$ remains (if it doesn't touch $bc$ — assume yes per problem).
$$\Delta_2 = 1 - L_1 = 1 - (-ad) = 1 + ad$$

**Step 5 — Apply Mason's Formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} &= \frac{P_1 \Delta_1 + P_2 \Delta_2}{\Delta} \\
&= \frac{a(1 + ce + bcf) + bc(1 + ad)}{1 + ad + ce + bcf + acde}
\end{aligned}$$

Expanding the numerator:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= a + ace + abcf + bc + abcd \\
&= a + bc + ace + abcf + abcd
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{a + bc + ace + abcf + abcd}{1 + ad + ce + bcf + acde}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Block Diagram Topology:**

The block diagram has:
- $G_1$ in forward path from $R(s)$
- $G_3$ as parallel feedforward from $R(s)$ (direct to summing junction)
- $G_2$ in series after summing junction
- $H_1$ as feedback from $C(s)$ to summing junction

**Step 2 — Construct Equivalent SFG:**

Nodes: $R$, $A$ (output of summing junction), $C$
Branches:
- $R \to A$ with gain $G_1$
- $R \to A$ with gain $G_3$ (parallel feedforward)
- $A \to C$ with gain $G_2$
- $C \to A$ with gain $-H_1$ (negative feedback)

**Step 3 — Identify Forward Paths:**

**Path 1:** $R \to A \to C$ (via $G_1$)
$$P_1 = G_1 G_2$$

**Path 2:** $R \to A \to C$ (via $G_3$)
$$P_2 = G_3 G_2$$

**Step 4 — Identify Loops:**

**Loop 1:** $A \to C \to A$
$$L_1 = (G_2)(-H_1) = -G_2 H_1$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\Delta$:**
$$\Delta = 1 - L_1 = 1 + G_2 H_1$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\Delta_k$:** Both paths touch all nodes of the loop.
$$\Delta_1 = \Delta_2 = 1$$

**Step 7 — Apply Mason's Formula:**
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_1 G_2 + G_3 G_2}{1 + G_2 H_1} = \frac{G_2(G_1 + G_3)}{1 + G_2 H_1}$$

This result can be **independently verified** using block diagram reduction: the parallel blocks $G_1$ and $G_3$ combine to $(G_1 + G_3)$, then form a closed loop with $G_2$ and $H_1$, yielding:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_2(G_1 + G_3)}{1 + G_2 H_1}$$

Both methods yield identical results, confirming the **equivalence** of block diagram reduction and Mason's Gain Formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for SFG (Module 1):**

- **SFG Core:** A graph of **nodes** (variables) connected by **directed branches** (transmittances).
- **Source Node:** Only outgoing branches; corresponds to **independent variable** ($R(s)$).
- **Sink Node:** Only incoming branches; corresponds to **dependent variable** ($C(s)$).
- **Path:** Sequence of branches in the indicated direction, **no node repeated**.
- **Forward Path:** Path from source to sink, no node repeated.
- **Loop:** Closed path with no repeated node.
- **Non-Touching Loops:** Loops sharing **zero common nodes**.
- **Mason's Gain Formula:** $T = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Determinant $\Delta$:** $1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \cdots$
- **Cofactor $\Delta_k$:** Determinant of the residual graph after **removing all nodes of $P_k$**.
- **Sign Convention:** Negative feedback yields transmittance of $-H(s)$ in the feedback branch.
- **Equivalent Methods:** Block diagram reduction and SFG + MGF give identical results.
- **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting non-touching loop products (especially $L_i L_j$ terms in $\Delta$).
- **Common Mistake 2:** Not recomputing $\Delta_k$ on the residual graph.
- **Common Mistake 3:** Missing the negative sign in negative-feedback loop transmittances.
- **Always Show Formula:** State Mason's Gain Formula at the start of the solution — earns 2 marks independently.
- **Final Answer Format:** Always express the transfer function as a single rational function $\dfrac{N(s)}{D(s)}$ with denominator in standard polynomial form.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling of Mechanical Systems: Translational and Rotational Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT631 - NETWORK AND LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modelling of Mechanical Systems: Translational and Rotational Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In control system engineering, **mathematical modelling of mechanical systems** refers to the systematic procedure of representing a physical mechanical assembly using a set of **linear time-invariant (LTI) differential equations** that capture the relationship between inputs (forces or torques), system parameters (mass, spring stiffness, damping coefficient, moment of inertia), and outputs (linear or angular displacement, velocity, or acceleration).

A **translational mechanical system** is one in which motion occurs along a straight line, governed by Newton's Second Law of Motion applied along the direction of motion:

$$F(t) = M \cdot \frac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}}$$

A **rotational mechanical system** is one in which motion occurs about a fixed axis, governed by the rotational equivalent of Newton's Second Law:

$$T(t) = J \cdot \frac{d^{2}\theta(t)}{dt^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The modelling exercise for Module 2 mandates that students must be able to: (i) draw **free body diagrams (FBD)**, (ii) write **force-balance or torque-balance equations** using D'Alembert's principle, and (iii) derive the **transfer function** $G(s) = \dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)}$ or $G(s) = \dfrac{\Theta(s)}{T(s)}$ assuming **zero initial conditions**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine pushing a **child on a swing**. When you apply a force, three things happen simultaneously:

1. The **chain (spring)** stretches and tries to pull the swing back.
2. The **air (damper)** resists the motion, slowing it down gradually.
3. The **seat + child (mass)** has inertia and resists sudden changes in motion.

A mechanical system model is simply a mathematical "recipe card" that quantifies exactly *how much* force is needed to overcome these three effects. In **translational systems**, motion is in a straight line (like a sliding drawer); in **rotational systems**, motion is circular (like a rotating motor shaft). The mathematics for both is structurally identical — only the variables change (force ↔ torque, mass ↔ moment of inertia, displacement ↔ angular displacement).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants & Units (SI):**
> - Mass ($M$): **kg** ; Force ($F$): **N** ; Displacement ($x$): **m**
> - Spring constant ($K$): **N/m** ; Damper coefficient ($B$): **N·s/m**
> - Moment of Inertia ($J$): **kg·m²** ; Torque ($T$): **N·m**
> - Angular displacement ($\theta$): **rad** ; Torsional stiffness ($K$): **N·m/rad**
> - Rotational damping ($B$): **N·m·s/rad**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Translational Mechanical Systems

### 2.1.1 The Mass Element ($M$)
- Stores **kinetic energy**; resists changes in velocity.
- Force–velocity relationship is governed by Newton's law:

$$F_M(t) = M \cdot \frac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}}$$

- In the $s$-domain (zero initial conditions):

$$F_M(s) = M \cdot s^{2} \cdot X(s)$$

- **Impedance analogue:** $Z_M(s) = M s^{2}$.

### 2.1.2 The Spring Element ($K$)
- Stores **potential energy**; provides a restoring force proportional to displacement.
- **Hooke's Law** governs its behavior:

$$F_K(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

- In the $s$-domain:

$$F_K(s) = K \cdot X(s)$$

- **Impedance analogue:** $Z_K(s) = K$.

### 2.1.3 The Viscous Damper ($B$)
- Dissipates energy as **heat**; provides a force proportional to velocity.
- Its defining equation:

$$F_B(t) = B \cdot \frac{dx(t)}{dt}$$

- In the $s$-domain:

$$F_B(s) = B \cdot s \cdot X(s)$$

- **Impedance analogue:** $Z_B(s) = B s$.

> [!TIP]
> **D'Alembert's Principle (for Force-Balance):** The algebraic sum of all forces acting on a rigid body in the direction of motion equals **zero** when inertial force $-M\ddot{x}$ is treated as an external force. This converts a dynamic problem into a static equilibrium problem, which is the standard exam-board method.

## 2.2 Components of Rotational Mechanical Systems

### 2.2.1 Moment of Inertia ($J$)
- Rotational analogue of mass; resists angular acceleration.

$$T_J(t) = J \cdot \frac{d^{2}\theta(t)}{dt^{2}}$$

### 2.2.2 Torsional Spring ($K$)
- Restoring torque proportional to angular displacement.

$$T_K(t) = K \cdot \theta(t)$$

### 2.2.3 Rotational Viscous Damper ($B$)
- Damping torque proportional to angular velocity.

$$T_B(t) = B \cdot \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt}$$

## 2.3 Mechanical Impedances — A Unified Operator View

In the Laplace domain, each element behaves as a "mechanical impedance" $Z(s)$ relating force/torque to displacement:

| Element | Translational Form | Rotational Form | Impedance $Z(s)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spring (Stiffness) | $F = Kx$ | $T = K\theta$ | $K$ |
| Damper (Friction) | $F = B\dot{x}$ | $T = B\dot{\theta}$ | $B s$ |
| Mass / Inertia | $F = M\ddot{x}$ | $T = J\ddot{\theta}$ | $M s^{2}$ or $J s^{2}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **opposition to motion** of any element is captured by the **mechanical impedance** $Z(s) = F(s)/X(s)$. This is the cornerstone concept that enables direct analogy with electrical impedances ($V/I = R$, $V/I = Ls$, $V/I = 1/Cs$).

## 2.4 Force–Voltage and Force–Current Analogies

To solve mechanical problems using electrical network theorems (Kirchhoff's laws), the following one-to-one mappings are used:

| Mechanical Quantity | Force–Voltage Analogy | Force–Current Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Force $F(t)$ / Torque $T(t)$ | Voltage $V(t)$ | Current $I(t)$ |
| Displacement $x(t)$ / Angle $\theta(t)$ | Charge $q(t)$ | Flux $\phi(t)$ |
| Velocity $\dot{x}$ / $\dot{\theta}$ | Current $i(t)$ | Voltage $v(t)$ |
| Mass $M$ / Inertia $J$ | Inductance $L$ | Capacitance $C$ |
| Spring $K$ | $1/$Capacitance | $1/$Inductance |
| Damper $B$ | Resistance $R$ | Conductance $1/R$ |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Modelling mechanical systems is foundational to designing:
- **Suspension systems** in automobiles (mass-spring-damper).
- **Robotic arms** (rotational joints with servo motors).
- **Hard-disk drive head positioning** (precision rotational control).
- **Aerospace control surfaces** and **vibration isolators**.
- **Building seismic dampers** for earthquake resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world note:** Every modern CNC machine tool relies on transfer functions of this exact form to position a tool-head to micrometre accuracy. A $1\%$ error in the modelled mass $M$ leads to an unacceptable overshoot in the step response.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass–Spring–Damper Translational System

**System Description:** A mass $M$ is connected to a fixed wall through a spring $K$ and a damper $B$ in parallel. An external force $F(t)$ is applied to the mass; the resulting displacement is $x(t)$ (measured from the equilibrium position).

### Step 1: Construct the Free Body Diagram (FBD)
Forces acting on mass $M$ in the positive $x$-direction:
- Applied external force: $+F(t)$
- Spring restoring force (opposing displacement): $-Kx(t)$
- Damper viscous force (opposing velocity): $-B\dot{x}(t)$
- Inertial reaction (D'Alembert): $-M\ddot{x}(t)$

### Step 2: Apply D'Alembert's Principle (Force-Balance Equation)
Summing all forces to zero:

$$F(t) - Kx(t) - B\dot{x}(t) - M\ddot{x}(t) = 0$$

### Step 3: Rearrange into Standard Form

$$M\ddot{x}(t) + B\dot{x}(t) + Kx(t) = F(t)$$

### Step 4: Take the Laplace Transform (Zero Initial Conditions)
Using $\mathcal{L}\{\ddot{x}\} = s^{2}X(s)$, $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}\} = sX(s)$, $\mathcal{L}\{x\} = X(s)$:

$$M s^{2} X(s) + B s X(s) + K X(s) = F(s)$$

### Step 5: Factor out $X(s)$ and Solve for the Transfer Function

$$\left( M s^{2} + B s + K \right) X(s) = F(s)$$

### Step 6: Express as Transfer Function $G(s)$

$$G(s) = \frac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1}{M s^{2} + B s + K}$$

**Interpretation:** This is a **standard second-order system** with natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$ rad/s and damping ratio $\zeta = B/(2\sqrt{MK})$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass–Spring–Damper with Two Masses (Two-DOF Translational System)

**System Description:** Two masses $M_1$ and $M_2$ are connected by a spring $K_2$ in series; mass $M_1$ is also connected to a fixed wall via spring $K_1$. Force $F(t)$ is applied to $M_1$, producing displacements $x_1$ and $x_2$.

### Step 1: FBD for Mass $M_1$
Forces on $M_1$:
- $+F(t)$ (external)
- $-K_1 x_1$ (spring $K_1$ pulls back)
- $-K_2(x_1 - x_2)$ (spring $K_2$ stretches if $x_1 > x_2$)
- $-B_1 \dot{x}_1$ (damper on $M_1$)
- $-M_1 \ddot{x}_1$ (inertia)

### Step 2: Equation for Mass $M_1$

$$M_1 \ddot{x}_1 + B_1 \dot{x}_1 + (K_1 + K_2) x_1 - K_2 x_2 = F(t)$$

### Step 3: FBD for Mass $M_2$
Forces on $M_2$:
- $-K_2(x_2 - x_1)$ (spring $K_2$ pulls back)
- $-B_2 \dot{x}_2$ (damper on $M_2$)
- $-M_2 \ddot{x}_2$ (inertia)

### Step 4: Equation for Mass $M_2$

$$M_2 \ddot{x}_2 + B_2 \dot{x}_2 + K_2 x_2 - K_2 x_1 = 0$$

### Step 5: Laplace Transform Both Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[ M_1 s^{2} + B_1 s + (K_1 + K_2) \right] X_1(s) - K_2 X_2(s) &= F(s) \\
- K_2 X_1(s) + \left[ M_2 s^{2} + B_2 s + K_2 \right] X_2(s) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Write in Matrix Form (Mechanical-Admittance Form)

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
M_1 s^{2} + B_1 s + K_1 + K_2 & -K_2 \\
-K_2 & M_2 s^{2} + B_2 s + K_2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} X_1(s) \\ X_2(s) \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix} F(s) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matrix equation can be solved using **Cramer's rule** to find $X_1(s)/F(s)$ or $X_2(s)/F(s)$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rotational System with Inertia, Torsional Spring, and Damper

**System Description:** A disc of moment of inertia $J$ is mounted on a shaft with torsional stiffness $K$. A rotational damper $B$ is also present. An external torque $T(t)$ produces angular displacement $\theta(t)$.

### Step 1: Apply Torque-Balance (Rotational D'Alembert's Principle)

$$T(t) - K\theta(t) - B\dot{\theta}(t) - J\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0$$

### Step 2: Rearrange

$$J\ddot{\theta}(t) + B\dot{\theta}(t) + K\theta(t) = T(t)$$

### Step 3: Laplace Transform

$$\left( J s^{2} + B s + K \right) \Theta(s) = T(s)$$

### Step 4: Transfer Function

$$G(s) = \frac{\Theta(s)}{T(s)} = \frac{1}{J s^{2} + B s + K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structural Isomorphism:** The transfer functions for a single-mass translational system and a single-inertia rotational system are **algebraically identical**. This is the reason for the **force–torque and mass–inertia analogy**, which is heavily tested in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gear-Train Coupled Rotational System

**System Description:** Two gears with $N_1$ and $N_2$ teeth couple a motor (inertia $J_1$, damping $B_1$) to a load (inertia $J_2$, damping $B_2$, torsional spring $K$).

### Step 1: Gear Ratio Definition

$$n = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = \frac{T_1}{T_2}$$

### Step 2: Reflect Load to Motor Shaft
Reflecting all load parameters to the motor shaft (shaft 1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{\text{eq}} &= J_1 + J_2 \cdot n^{2} \\
B_{\text{eq}} &= B_1 + B_2 \cdot n^{2} \\
K_{\text{eq}} &= K \cdot n^{2} \\
T_{\text{eq}} &= T - T_{\text{load,ref}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Effective Transfer Function at Motor Shaft

$$\frac{\theta_1(s)}{T(s)} = \frac{1}{J_{\text{eq}} s^{2} + B_{\text{eq}} s + K_{\text{eq}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam shortcut:** Whenever a gear-train is present, the easiest approach is to **reflect all components to a single shaft** using the gear-ratio-squared rule. This collapses a multi-shaft system into a single-inertia system.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Translational Mass-Spring-Damper

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Applied Force F t"] --> B["Mechanical Network Parallel Combination"]
    B --> C1["Mass M Impedance Ms squared"]
    B --> C2["Spring K Impedance K"]
    B --> C3["Damper B Impedance Bs"]
    C1 --> D["Displacement X t"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E["Transfer Function Gs equals 1 over Ms squared plus Bs plus K"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rotational Inertia-Torsional Spring-Damper

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["Input Torque T t"] --> B1["Rotational Mechanical Network"]
    B1 --> C4["Moment of Inertia J Impedance Js squared"]
    B1 --> C5["Torsional Spring K Impedance K"]
    B1 --> C6["Rotational Damper B Impedance Bs"]
    C4 --> D1["Angular Displacement Theta t"]
    C5 --> D1
    C6 --> D1
    D1 --> E1["Transfer Function Gs equals 1 over Js squared plus Bs plus K"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two-Degree-of-Freedom Mechanical System

```mermaid
graph TD
    Fin["External Force Fs"] --> M1eq["Mass M1 plus Damper B1 plus Springs K1 K2"]
    M1eq --> X1["Displacement X1 s"]
    X1 --> K2link["Coupling Spring K2 Acts on M2"]
    M2node["Mass M2 plus Damper B2 plus Spring K2"] --> X2["Displacement X2 s"]
    K2link --> M2node
    X1 --> MatOut["Matrix Equation AX equals B"]
    X2 --> MatOut
    MatOut --> Sol["Solve Using Cramers Rule to obtain Transfer Functions X1 by F and X2 by F"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force-Voltage Analogy

```mermaid
graph LR
    F["Force F t"] --> Volt["Voltage V t"]
    X["Displacement X t"] --> Chrg["Charge q t"]
    Velo["Velocity x dot"] --> Curr["Current i t"]
    MassNode["Mass M"] --> Ind["Inductance L"]
    SpringNode["Spring K"] --> Cap["Capacitance 1 over K"]
    DamperNode["Damper B"] --> Res["Resistance R"]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Gear-Train System

```mermaid
graph LR
    Motor["Motor Shaft Inertia J1 B1"] --> GR["Gear Ratio n equals N1 by N2"]
    GR --> Load["Load Shaft Inertia J2 B2 plus Torsional Spring K"]
    Reflect["Reflect Load to Motor Shaft using n squared rule"] --> Motor
    GR --> Reflect
    Load --> Reflect
    Reflect --> TF["Equivalent Transfer Function at Motor Shaft"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define mechanical impedance. List the impedances of mass, spring, and damper in the Laplace domain.**

**Model Answer:**
Mechanical impedance $Z(s)$ is the Laplace-domain ratio of force to displacement, $Z(s) = F(s)/X(s)$. It quantifies the opposition offered by an element to motion.
- Mass: $Z_M(s) = M s^{2}$
- Spring: $Z_K(s) = K$
- Damper: $Z_B(s) = B s$

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: State D'Alembert's principle as applied to a translational mechanical system. Why is it useful for system modelling?**

**Model Answer:**
D'Alembert's principle states that the algebraic sum of all real forces plus the inertial force (treated as a fictitious external force) acting on a body in the direction of motion equals zero. Mathematically:

$$F_{\text{applied}} - Kx - B\dot{x} - M\ddot{x} = 0$$

It is useful because it converts a **dynamic** problem into a **static equilibrium** problem, enabling direct application of algebraic methods (and hence transfer function derivation) without solving differential equations from scratch.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A mechanical system consists of a mass $M$ kg connected to a fixed wall via a spring $K$ N/m and damper $B$ N·s/m in parallel. An external force $F(t)$ acts on the mass. Derive the transfer function relating the displacement $x(t)$ to the applied force. Also identify the standard second-order parameters $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Derive the Differential Equation and Transfer Function

**Step 1 — FBD and Force-Balance:**
Sum of forces on $M$ = 0 (D'Alembert):

$$F(t) - Kx(t) - B\dot{x}(t) - M\ddot{x}(t) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Standard Form:**

$$M\ddot{x}(t) + B\dot{x}(t) + Kx(t) = F(t)$$

**Step 3 — Laplace Transform (zero ICs):**

$$\left( M s^{2} + B s + K \right) X(s) = F(s)$$

**Step 4 — Transfer Function:**

$$\boxed{\frac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1}{M s^{2} + B s + K}}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Identify $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$

Compare with standard form $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^{2}$.

Divide numerator and denominator by $M$:

$$\frac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1/M}{s^{2} + \dfrac{B}{M} s + \dfrac{K}{M}}$$

By comparison:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{M}} \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}}$$

**Numerical example:** If $M = 2$ kg, $B = 4$ N·s/m, $K = 8$ N/m, then $\omega_n = 2$ rad/s and $\zeta = 0.5$ (under-damped).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**For the two-mass translational system shown (mass $M_1$ connected to fixed wall by spring $K_1$, and to mass $M_2$ by spring $K_2$; damper $B_1$ acts on $M_1$ and damper $B_2$ on $M_2$; external force $F(t)$ acts on $M_1$), find the transfer function $X_1(s)/F(s)$.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Derive the Coupled Differential Equations

**For Mass $M_1$:**

$$M_1 \ddot{x}_1 + B_1 \dot{x}_1 + K_1 x_1 + K_2 (x_1 - x_2) = F(t)$$

**For Mass $M_2$:**

$$M_2 \ddot{x}_2 + B_2 \dot{x}_2 + K_2 (x_2 - x_1) = 0$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Solve Using Matrix/Cramer's Method

**Laplace-domain matrix:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
M_1 s^{2} + B_1 s + K_1 + K_2 & -K_2 \\
-K_2 & M_2 s^{2} + B_2 s + K_2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} X_1(s) \\ X_2(s) \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix} F(s) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Determinant of the coefficient matrix:**

$$\Delta = \left( M_1 s^{2} + B_1 s + K_1 + K_2 \right)\left( M_2 s^{2} + B_2 s + K_2 \right) - K_2^{2}$$

**Numerator (replacing first column with RHS vector):**

$$\Delta_1 = F(s) \cdot \left( M_2 s^{2} + B_2 s + K_2 \right)$$

**Transfer Function:**

$$\frac{X_1(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{\Delta_1}{\Delta} = \frac{M_2 s^{2} + B_2 s + K_2}{\left( M_1 s^{2} + B_1 s + K_1 + K_2 \right)\left( M_2 s^{2} + B_2 s + K_2 \right) - K_2^{2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Errors:** Students frequently forget the sign of the coupling spring force. If $x_1 > x_2$, the spring $K_2$ is stretched and pulls $M_1$ back (negative) but pulls $M_2$ forward (positive). Always draw the FBD.
> 2. **Missing Units:** Inertia must be in kg·m² (rotational) and not confused with kg.
> 3. **Initial Conditions:** Transfer functions are valid ONLY for zero initial conditions. State this assumption explicitly in your derivation step.
> 4. **Gear-train Reflection:** Forgetting to multiply load-side parameters by $n^{2}$ is the most common error (loses 2–3 marks).
> 5. **Skipping Laplace Transform Justification:** Write "$X(s) = \mathcal{L}\{x(t)\}$, assuming zero initial conditions" before each transform.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three foundational translational elements:** Mass ($M s^{2}$), Spring ($K$), Damper ($B s$).
- **Three rotational counterparts:** Inertia ($J s^{2}$), Torsional Spring ($K$), Rotational Damper ($B s$).
- **D'Alembert's principle** converts dynamics into a static force-balance equation — *always* begin problems with this.
- **Standard form** of a single-DOF translational/rotational transfer function:

$$\frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}} = \frac{1}{M s^{2} + B s + K} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{1}{J s^{2} + B s + K}$$

- **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$ or $\sqrt{K/J}$.
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}}$ or $\dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{JK}}$.
- **Force–Voltage analogy:** $F \leftrightarrow V$, $x \leftrightarrow q$, $M \leftrightarrow L$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/C$, $B \leftrightarrow R$.
- **Force–Current analogy:** $F \leftrightarrow I$, $x \leftrightarrow \phi$, $M \leftrightarrow C$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/L$, $B \leftrightarrow 1/R$.
- **Gear-train rule:** Reflect all load-side parameters to motor shaft multiplied by $n^{2}$.
- **Multi-DOF systems:** Always write in **matrix form** and solve via **Cramer's rule**.
- **Exam mantra:** Draw FBD → Apply D'Alembert → Laplace transform → Solve for transfer function.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transfer Function for Typical Mechanical Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT631 - NETWORK AND LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transfer Function for Typical Mechanical Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Transfer Function for Typical Mechanical Systems

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

In control system engineering, a **Mechanical System** is defined as a physical arrangement of inertial, elastic, and dissipative elements that store and exchange energy through **translation** (linear motion) or **rotation** (angular motion). The **Transfer Function (TF)** of such a system is the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output displacement (or velocity, or acceleration) to the Laplace transform of the input force (or torque), under the assumption of **zero initial conditions**.

Mathematically, for a mechanical system:

$$G(s) = \frac{\mathcal{L}\{\text{Output}(t)\}}{\mathcal{L}\{\text{Input}(t)\}} \bigg|_{Zero\,IC}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEERT631, Module 2):**
> The student must be able to derive the transfer function of:
> 1. **Mechanical Translational Systems** (Mass-Spring-Damper configurations)
> 2. **Mechanical Rotational Systems** (Inertia-Torsion Spring-Damper configurations)
> 3. **Mechanical Analogous Systems** (Force-Voltage and Force-Current analogies)

### 1.2 Fundamental Elements of Mechanical Systems

| Element | Translational Symbol | Rotational Symbol | Physical Role | Energy Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inertia | **Mass (M)** | **Moment of Inertia (J)** | Stores Kinetic Energy | Energy Storage |
| Damper | **Friction Coefficient (B)** | **Rotational Friction (B)** | Dissipates Energy as Heat | Energy Dissipative |
| Spring | **Stiffness (K)** | **Torsional Stiffness (K)** | Stores Potential Energy | Energy Storage |
| Input | Force **F(t)** | Torque **T(t)** | External Excitation | Energy Source |
| Output | Displacement **x(t)** | Angular Displacement **θ(t)** | System Response | Energy Sink |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Car Suspension System:**
> Imagine you are driving a car over a bumpy road. The car body is the **mass (M)**, the shock absorber is the **damper (B)**, and the coil spring is the **spring (K)**. The road bump applies a **force (F)**, and the car's vertical bounce is the **displacement (x)**. The transfer function captures *exactly* how the car's vertical position responds to every bump, in the frequency domain. **The mass "wants to keep moving," the spring "wants to return to rest," and the damper "wants to slow things down"** — the TF quantifies the tug-of-war between these three tendencies.

The fundamental governing law for every mechanical element is **Newton's Second Law**:

$$F_{net}(t) = M \cdot a(t) = M \cdot \frac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}}$$

and for rotational systems (Newton's rotational analogue):

$$T_{net}(t) = J \cdot \alpha(t) = J \cdot \frac{d^{2}\theta(t)}{dt^{2}}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Differential Equations

The transfer function derivation follows a strict 4-step protocol expected by KTU examiners:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Derivation Protocol:**
> 1. **Assume a node displacement** (e.g., x or θ) — this becomes the output.
> 2. **Identify all forces/torques** acting on the node (applied + reaction from spring + reaction from damper + inertia force).
> 3. **Apply D'Alembert's Principle** (dynamic equilibrium): ΣF = 0 or ΣT = 0.
> 4. **Take the Laplace Transform** with zero initial conditions and algebraically isolate Output/Input.

### 2.2 Force-Voltage Analogy (F-V Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently asked analogy** in KTU exams for Module 2.

| Mechanical Element | Electrical Equivalent | Governing Law |
|---|---|---|
| Force **F** | Voltage **V** | Across Variable |
| Velocity **ẋ** | Current **i** | Through Variable |
| Mass **M** | Inductance **L** | $F = M \frac{d\dot{x}}{dt} \;\leftrightarrow\; V = L \frac{di}{dt}$ |
| Damper **B** | Resistance **R** | $F = B\dot{x} \;\leftrightarrow\; V = Ri$ |
| Spring **K** | Inverse Capacitance **1/C** | $F = K \int \dot{x}\, dt \;\leftrightarrow\; V = \frac{1}{C}\int i\, dt$ |

### 2.3 Force-Current Analogy (F-I Analogy)

| Mechanical Element | Electrical Equivalent | Governing Law |
|---|---|---|
| Force **F** | Current **I** | Through Variable |
| Velocity **ẋ** | Voltage **V** | Across Variable |
| Mass **M** | Capacitance **C** | $F = M \frac{d\dot{x}}{dt} \;\leftrightarrow\; I = C \frac{dV}{dt}$ |
| Damper **B** | Inverse Resistance **1/R** | $F = B\dot{x} \;\leftrightarrow\; I = \frac{V}{R}$ |
| Spring **K** | Inverse Inductance **1/L** | $F = K \int \dot{x}\, dt \;\leftrightarrow\; I = \frac{1}{L}\int V\, dt$ |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

The following consolidated table is the **single most important reference** for solving any KTU Module 2 numerical on Mechanical Systems.

| System Configuration | Transfer Function | Output Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Single Mass-Spring-Damper (Translation) | $\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Ms^{2} + Bs + K}$ | Displacement |
| Single Mass-Spring-Damper (Velocity out) | $\dfrac{\dot{X}(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{s}{Ms^{2} + Bs + K}$ | Velocity |
| Single Inertia-Torsion-Damper (Rotation) | $\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{T(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Js^{2} + Bs + K}$ | Angular Displacement |
| Two Coupled Masses (M₁ coupled to M₂ via K, B) | Complex — see Section 3.2 derivation | Depends on node chosen |
| Mass on frictionless surface with spring & damper in parallel | $\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Ms^{2} + (B_{1}+B_{2})s + (K_{1}+K_{2})}$ | Displacement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Verification (must memorize):**
> - **M** → kg (kilogram)
> - **B** → N·s/m (Newton-second per meter)
> - **K** → N/m (Newton per meter)
> - **J** → kg·m² (kilogram-meter squared)
> - **F** → N (Newton)
> - **T** → N·m (Newton-meter)
> - **x** → m (meter)
> - **θ** → rad (radian — dimensionless but tracked for clarity)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mechanical transfer functions are the **backbone** of:

- **Automotive Suspension Design** — Ride comfort and road-holding analysis.
- **Robotics Manipulator Arms** — Joint torque to angular displacement.
- **Aircraft Control Surfaces** — Aileron/elevator response to actuator commands.
- **Seismic Isolation of Buildings** — Earthquake input → floor displacement.
- **Hard Disk Drive Read Heads** — Head-positioning servo control.
- **Wind Turbine Blade Dynamics** — Pitch control to suppress vibration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters for KTU:**
> Module 2 is a **gateway module** for Module 3 (Time Response) and Module 4 (Stability). Every closed-loop stability criterion (Routh-Hurwitz, Root Locus, Bode) is evaluated on the TF you derive here. A single sign error in Section 3 propagation invalidates the entire downstream analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Standard:**
> Every algebraic step must be visible. Marks are awarded per logical transition. Skipping a step (e.g., writing "by Laplace transform") is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single Mass-Spring-Damper Translational System

**System Description:**
A mass **M** is connected to a fixed wall via a spring of stiffness **K** and a damper of coefficient **B**, all in parallel. An external force **F(t)** acts on the mass, producing a displacement **x(t)**.

### Step 1 — Free Body Identification
Let us assume the mass moves in the positive x-direction under the action of F(t).

Forces acting on the mass:
1. **Applied Force:** $F(t)$ (acts to the right, +x direction)
2. **Spring Restoring Force:** $-K \cdot x(t)$ (opposes displacement, $-x$ direction)
3. **Damper Friction Force:** $-B \cdot \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ (opposes velocity)
4. **Inertia Force (D'Alembert):** $-M \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}}$ (opposes acceleration)

### Step 2 — Apply D'Alembert's Principle (Dynamic Equilibrium)
By D'Alembert's principle, the sum of all real forces plus the inertia force equals zero:

$$F(t) - K \cdot x(t) - B \cdot \frac{dx(t)}{dt} - M \cdot \frac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}} = 0$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange into Standard Form
Move the input force to the right-hand side and the displacement terms to the left-hand side:

$$M \cdot \frac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}} + B \cdot \frac{dx(t)}{dt} + K \cdot x(t) = F(t)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Laplace Transform (Zero Initial Conditions)
Using the standard Laplace identities:

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}}\right\} = s^{2}X(s) - s\,x(0) - \dot{x}(0) = s^{2}X(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = sX(s) - x(0) = sX(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\{x(t)\} = X(s), \quad \mathcal{L}\{F(t)\} = F(s)$$

Substituting these into the differential equation:

$$M \cdot s^{2}X(s) + B \cdot sX(s) + K \cdot X(s) = F(s)$$

### Step 5 — Factor Out X(s) and Isolate the Transfer Function

$$\begin{aligned}
X(s) \cdot \left[ Ms^{2} + Bs + K \right] &= F(s) \\
G(s) = \frac{X(s)}{F(s)} &= \frac{1}{Ms^{2} + Bs + K}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **canonical second-order transfer function** of a single-mass translational system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> The denominator $Ms^{2} + Bs + K$ is the **characteristic polynomial**. Its roots (poles) are:
> - $s = -\dfrac{B}{2M} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{B}{2M}\right)^{2} - \dfrac{K}{M}}$
> - These poles determine whether the system is **overdamped, critically damped, or underdamped** — a direct linkage to Module 3 (Time Response).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Two-Mass Coupled Translational System

**System Description:**
Two masses **M₁** and **M₂** are connected in series. M₁ is connected to a fixed wall via spring **K₁** and damper **B₁**. Between M₁ and M₂, there is a coupling spring **K₂** and coupling damper **B₂**. An external force **F(t)** acts on M₁. The output displacement is **x₂(t)** on M₂.

### Step 1 — Define Node Displacements
Let $x_1(t)$ be the displacement of M₁ and $x_2(t)$ be the displacement of M₂.

### Step 2 — Equation of Motion for Mass M₁

**Forces on M₁:**
- Applied force: $+F(t)$
- Wall spring: $-K_1 x_1(t)$
- Wall damper: $-B_1 \dot{x}_1(t)$
- Coupling spring (extended by $x_1 - x_2$): $-K_2 [x_1(t) - x_2(t)]$
- Coupling damper: $-B_2 [\dot{x}_1(t) - \dot{x}_2(t)]$
- Inertia: $-M_1 \ddot{x}_1(t)$

Applying D'Alembert's principle and summing to zero:

$$M_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (B_1 + B_2)\dot{x}_1 - B_2 \dot{x}_2 + (K_1 + K_2)x_1 - K_2 x_2 = F(t)$$

### Step 3 — Equation of Motion for Mass M₂

**Forces on M₂:**
- Coupling spring reaction: $+K_2 [x_1(t) - x_2(t)]$ (pulls M₂ toward M₁)
- Coupling damper reaction: $+B_2 [\dot{x}_1(t) - \dot{x}_2(t)]$
- Inertia: $-M_2 \ddot{x}_2(t)$

Applying D'Alembert's principle:

$$M_2 \ddot{x}_2 + B_2 \dot{x}_2 - B_2 \dot{x}_1 + K_2 x_2 - K_2 x_1 = 0$$

### Step 4 — Take the Laplace Transform of Both Equations

Equation 1 in the s-domain:

$$[M_1 s^{2} + (B_1 + B_2)s + (K_1 + K_2)]X_1(s) - [B_2 s + K_2]X_2(s) = F(s)$$

Equation 2 in the s-domain:

$$-[B_2 s + K_2]X_1(s) + [M_2 s^{2} + B_2 s + K_2]X_2(s) = 0$$

### Step 5 — Solve the System via Cramer's Rule (or Matrix Inversion)

Write in matrix form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{B}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} M_1 s^{2} + (B_1 + B_2)s + (K_1 + K_2) & -(B_2 s + K_2) \\ -(B_2 s + K_2) & M_2 s^{2} + B_2 s + K_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_1(s) \\ X_2(s) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} F(s) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

The determinant of the coefficient matrix (the **system characteristic determinant**) is:

$$\Delta = [M_1 s^{2} + (B_1 + B_2)s + (K_1 + K_2)] \cdot [M_2 s^{2} + B_2 s + K_2] - (B_2 s + K_2)^{2}$$

To find $X_2(s)$ using Cramer's rule, replace the second column with $\mathbf{B}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{X_2} &= \begin{vmatrix} M_1 s^{2} + (B_1 + B_2)s + (K_1 + K_2) & F(s) \\ -(B_2 s + K_2) & 0 \end{vmatrix} \\
&= F(s) \cdot (B_2 s + K_2)
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$X_2(s) = \frac{\Delta_{X_2}}{\Delta} = \frac{F(s) \cdot (B_2 s + K_2)}{[M_1 s^{2} + (B_1 + B_2)s + (K_1 + K_2)] \cdot [M_2 s^{2} + B_2 s + K_2] - (B_2 s + K_2)^{2}}$$

The transfer function is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{X_2(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{B_2 s + K_2}{M_1 M_2 s^{4} + [M_1 B_2 + M_2(B_1+B_2)]s^{3} + [M_1 K_2 + M_2(K_1+K_2) + B_1 B_2]s^{2} + [B_1 K_2 + B_2 K_1]s + K_1 K_2}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Single Rotational System

**System Description:**
A disk with moment of inertia **J** is mounted on a shaft. The shaft has torsional stiffness **K** and rotational friction coefficient **B**. An external torque **T(t)** is applied, and the angular displacement is **θ(t)**.

### Step 1 — Identify Torques on the Disk
1. Applied torque: $+T(t)$
2. Torsional spring torque: $-K \cdot \theta(t)$
3. Rotational friction torque: $-B \cdot \dot{\theta}(t)$
4. Inertia torque (D'Alembert): $-J \cdot \ddot{\theta}(t)$

### Step 2 — Dynamic Equilibrium

$$T(t) - K\theta(t) - B\dot{\theta}(t) - J\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0$$

$$J\ddot{\theta}(t) + B\dot{\theta}(t) + K\theta(t) = T(t)$$

### Step 3 — Laplace Transform

$$Js^{2}\Theta(s) + Bs\Theta(s) + K\Theta(s) = T(s)$$

### Step 4 — Isolate the Transfer Function

$$\frac{\Theta(s)}{T(s)} = \frac{1}{Js^{2} + Bs + K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Structural Isomorphism Notice:**
> The rotational transfer function $\dfrac{1}{Js^{2} + Bs + K}$ has **exactly the same mathematical form** as the translational transfer function $\dfrac{1}{Ms^{2} + Bs + K}$. The only substitution is $M \leftrightarrow J$ and $F \leftrightarrow T$, $x \leftrightarrow \theta$. This **isomorphism** is the foundation of the force-voltage/force-current analogies and is a **favorite KTU question**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Master Comparison Matrix

| Configuration | Order (n) | Poles | Zeros | Key Coupling Term |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single Mass-Spring-Damper (Translation) | 2 | 2 real or complex conjugate | 0 | None (no coupling) |
| Single Rotational System | 2 | 2 real or complex conjugate | 0 | None (no coupling) |
| Two-Mass Coupled System | 4 | Up to 4 (depends on damping) | 1 (at $s = -K_2/B_2$) | $(B_2 s + K_2)$ in numerator |
| Gear-Train Coupled Rotational System | 2 to 4 | Depends on gear ratio $N$ | Depends | Reflected inertia/damping through $N^{2}$ |
